function.arguments: {"question": "What are the quantitative results (wirelength, congestion, DRC violations, runtime) and limitations of deep reinforcement learning for chip macro placement, including Google's Circuit Training, its critiques by Cheng et al., and alternative approaches like ChiPFormer, DREAMPlace, and Bayesian optimization?"}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000000

From text 'cheng2025anupdatedassessment pages 12-13'.

Chung-Kuan Cheng, Andrew B. Kahng, Sayak Kundu, Yucheng Wang, and Zhiang Wang. An updated assessment of reinforcement learning for macro placement. IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems, pages 1-1, Jan 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1109/tcad.2025.3644293, doi:10.1109/tcad.2025.3644293. This article has 45 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

Cheng et al. (2025) re-evaluate Google’s Circuit Training (AlphaChip) and find several quantitative and practical limitations. They show pre-training can enable convergence on larger netlists that fail from-scratch (example: CT-Ariane-X4, 532 macros, TSMC 7nm), but pre-training outcomes depend strongly on dataset diversity and the size of the largest slice; some pre-training jobs diverge. In their experiments, a strengthened simulated-annealing (SA) baseline (multi-threaded + "go-with-the-winners") outperforms AlphaChip on the large CT-Ariane-X4 testcase in post-detailed placement HPWL and in the CT proxy cost while using a fraction of the runtime and compute. They also observe that the proxy cost optimized by CT correlates weakly with final post-route PPA metrics, raising concerns about using CT’s proxy as a ground truth. Training returns for some pre-trained models show sharp early gains (e.g., at 50 hours) but little improvement thereafter. The authors note stability, stochasticity, scalability, and compute/runtime demands as significant limitations, and that independent reproduction of the Nature/AlphaChip claims had not been reported by November 2025. The excerpt does not provide exact numeric wirelength, congestion, DRC-violation counts, or runtimes for all methods, nor detailed quantitative comparisons for alternative approaches such as ChiPFormer, DREAMPlace, or Bayesian optimization.


---

## pqac-00000001

From text 'cheng2021onjointlearning pages 7-9'.

Ruoyu Cheng and Junchi Yan. On joint learning for solving placement and routing in chip design. Preprint, Jan 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2111.00234, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2111.00234. This article has 163 citations.

Cheng et al. (DeepPlace / DeepPR) evaluate an RL-based joint macro/standard-cell placement and placement+routing pipeline using PPO (PyTorch), GCN backbone, Adam (lr 2.5e-4), and DREAMPlace to handle standard-cell legalization/optimization. Experiments run on a single RTX 2080Ti; the environment is deterministic so error bars were neglected. Pretraining the policy on a macro-only partial netlist speeds training: pretraining ~300 iterations gave best downstream performance, whereas excessive pretraining (e.g., 500 iters) harmed exploration and caused overfitting; training from scratch was less stable and slower because most runtime is gradient-based placement per episode. Quantitatively (Table 2), DeepPlace achieves lower wirelengths than a sequential learning placer and often better than DREAMPlace; example numbers: adaptec1: DeepPlace 80,117,232 vs sequential 86,675,688 vs DREAMPlace 83,749,616; bigblue4: DeepPlace 951,984,128 vs sequential 1,039,976,064 (DREAMPlace failed congestion checks). Overall runtime shows ~4× speedup over RePlace. For joint placement+routing (DeepPR), optimizing directly for routed wirelength substantially improves final routing wirelength versus pipelines that optimize HPWL, indicating HPWL can be a poor proxy for routed wirelength. Limitations noted: susceptibility to overfitting with excessive pretraining; pretraining objective (macro-only wirelength) does not guarantee global optimum for full placement; DREAMPlace sometimes fails congestion checks when treating macros as general cells.


---

## pqac-00000002

From text 'lai2023chipformertransferablechip pages 18-20'.

Yao Lai, Jinxin Liu, Zhentao Tang, Bin Wang, Jianye Hao, and Ping Luo. Chipformer: transferable chip placement via offline decision transformer. ArXiv, Jun 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2306.14744, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2306.14744. This article has 120 citations.

This excerpt does not provide quantitative metrics (wirelength, congestion, DRC violations, or runtimes) for Google’s Circuit Training or other methods. It summarizes methods and limitations: RL-based placement approaches have primarily been online methods (e.g., GraphPlace/Circuit-Training style using policy gradients and GCN encoders), typically place hard macros first and rely on subsequent solvers (force-directed or DREAMPlace) for soft macros. DREAMPlace (a non-quadratic analytic solver) is noted to yield better results than force-directed methods. Critiques cited include DeepPR/PRNet failing to account for actual macro sizes, causing inaccurate HPWL rewards and unavoidable overlaps. MaskPlace’s CNN-based visual representations omit circuit topology and previous state information, tending toward sub-optimal solutions. Simulated annealing is noted as computationally inefficient. The paper introduces ChiPFormer as an offline decision-transformer approach and lists hyperparameters and hardware used (e.g., AMD Ryzen 9 5950X, 2× RTX 3090, training/finetuning learning rates, phase-2 iterations = 300), but provides no placement quality numbers here. No information about Bayesian optimization performance is present in this excerpt.


---

## pqac-00000003

From text 'cheng2025anupdatedassessment pages 1-2'.

Chung-Kuan Cheng, Andrew B. Kahng, Sayak Kundu, Yucheng Wang, and Zhiang Wang. An updated assessment of reinforcement learning for macro placement. IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems, pages 1-1, Jan 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1109/tcad.2025.3644293, doi:10.1109/tcad.2025.3644293. This article has 45 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

This excerpt (Cheng et al., 2025) summarizes a careful re-evaluation of Google Brain’s Circuit Training (CT) for macro placement and emphasizes reproducibility concerns. The authors note that reproduction and evaluation of Nature and CT have been hampered because neither the data nor the code was fully available, and that irreproducibility led to controversy. Prior critiques (Goldie et al. / related posts) called out issues including absence of peer review, questions about RL pre-training, potential non-convergence on some testcases, mismatch of technology nodes used (45nm/12nm vs. sub-10nm like TSMC 7nm), and insufficient compute. Cheng et al. state they train the updated Circuit Training from scratch and fine-tune Google’s August 2024 “AlphaChip” checkpoint (CT-AC), and they strengthen the simulated annealing (SA) baseline using the “go-with-the-winners” metaheuristic and multithreading. They also mention use of a place-and-route tool to obtain a “true reward” post-route, and that all data, flows, and scripts are publicly released in the MacroPlacement GitHub. The excerpt does not contain quantitative metrics (wirelength, congestion, DRC violations, or runtimes); it reports methodology, critique points, and that stronger SA baselines and reproducible benchmarks were produced for evaluation.


---

## pqac-00000004

From text 'cheng2021onjointlearning pages 9-12'.

Ruoyu Cheng and Junchi Yan. On joint learning for solving placement and routing in chip design. Preprint, Jan 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2111.00234, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2111.00234. This article has 163 citations.

Cheng et al. (DeepPR / DeepPlace) report joint placement-and-routing results on ISPD-2005 benchmarks showing lower final wirelengths than a separate pipeline: e.g., DeepPR wirelengths (per-benchmark) 5298, 22256, 32839, 63560, 8602, 16819, 94083, 17585 vs Separate Pipeline 6077, 23725, 37029, 79573, 9078, 17849, 98480, 17829. Inference-time runtimes per benchmark are listed (e.g., 10.63s, 10.66s, 40.96s, 98.23s, 16.12s, 32.71s, 77.60s, 18.73s) and authors note inference cost is comparable between methods. They observe congestion issues in prior work (RePlace) "due to overlap between macros," and that their RL agent behavior (placing macros in-net close together and choosing nearest valid replacements on overlap) can lead to a diamond-shaped placement pattern. Ablations show combining CNN (global) and GNN (detailed node) embeddings improves learning, and adding Random Network Distillation (RND) intrinsic reward substantially boosts exploration and episodic returns. Limitations: the model is "limited to a moderate number of macros" because of GNN scalability and large RL action spaces; macro size/shape and human heuristics are not fully handled and are suggested improvements. The excerpt does not provide explicit congestion metrics, DRC-violation counts, or comparisons to Google Circuit Training, ChiPFormer, DREAMPlace, or Bayesian optimization beyond citing related work.

function.arguments: {"question": "What are the quantitative results and methods for PCB component placement automation, PCB routing, FanoutNet, and deep reinforcement learning approaches for PCB design? Include wirelength improvement, routability, completion rate, and runtime metrics."}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000005

From text 'li2023fanoutnetaneuralized pages 7-8'.

Haiyun Li, Jixin Zhang, Ning Xu, and Mingyu Liu. Fanoutnet: a neuralized pcb fanout automation method using deep reinforcement learning. Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence, 37:8554-8561, Jun 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1609/aaai.v37i7.26030, doi:10.1609/aaai.v37i7.26030. This article has 15 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports quantitative evaluation of FanoutNet, a neuralized PCB fanout method using reinforcement learning (PPO) and a multi-constraint router. On an open-source dataset (Table 5) FanoutNet achieved 100% completion/routability across the listed benchmarks. Average metrics (last column) for FanoutNet on that dataset: via count avg = 25, wire length (WL) avg = 1,160 mm, and runtime T avg = 50 s. For comparison the excerpt lists competing results (e.g., FR WL avg = 1,456 mm, EL WL avg = 1,595 mm; FR #Via avg = 26, EL #Via avg = 50). On an industrial dataset (Table 6) FanoutNet also achieved 100% RT(%). Industrial averages for FanoutNet are: #Via = 270, WL = 433 (units in table header), and runtime T(avg) = 245 minutes. The text explicitly states the method "significantly improves routability, wire length, and via count compared with state-of-the-art methods." The paper encodes spatial/structured features, optimizes fanout via PPO, and uses a verification router to assess final routing quality.


---

## pqac-00000006

From text 'li2023fanoutnetaneuralized pages 4-6'.

Haiyun Li, Jixin Zhang, Ning Xu, and Mingyu Liu. Fanoutnet: a neuralized pcb fanout automation method using deep reinforcement learning. Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence, 37:8554-8561, Jun 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1609/aaai.v37i7.26030, doi:10.1609/aaai.v37i7.26030. This article has 15 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes FanoutNet’s reinforcement-learning based fanout method combined with a Multi-Constraint Router (MCR). The network uses attention layers (MHA + FF) and an MLP policy/value head trained with PPO (K epochs = 20; learning rates 3e-4 and 1e-3). The MCR frames routing as a constrained 3-D A* search with cost ˆf(n)=ˆg(n)+ˆh(n); sub-costs include wire-length w(n)=∥nc−n∥2, layer-change cost v(n)=|z(nc)−z(n)|, and additional terms for differential pairs, reference-layer shielding, and spacing. The combined ˆg uses coefficients c1..c5 adjusted during iterations. Experiments use Python/PyTorch for RL and C++ (mingw64) for routing on an AMD Ryzen 7 5800X workstation. Benchmarks include 11 open-source and 5 industrial PCB cases. Evaluation metrics reported are Routability (RT), Via Count, Wire Length (WL), Runtime (T), and Pre-Routability for training. Comparative methods listed are Freerouting2, ELECTRA, NB-A*, GA, and ARL. The excerpt contains one quantitative routability fragment for MCR across cases: "MCR 100 85 98 99 97 RT (%)" but does not provide full, detailed numeric comparisons (wirelength improvements, completion rates, or runtime figures) between FanoutNet/deep-RL and the other methods within the provided text.


---

## pqac-00000007

From text 'li2023fanoutnetaneuralized pages 6-7'.

Haiyun Li, Jixin Zhang, Ning Xu, and Mingyu Liu. Fanoutnet: a neuralized pcb fanout automation method using deep reinforcement learning. Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence, 37:8554-8561, Jun 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1609/aaai.v37i7.26030, doi:10.1609/aaai.v37i7.26030. This article has 15 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports quantitative routing results for FanoutNet (an RL-based fanout method) versus baselines. FanoutNet "obtains 100% routability in all PCB cases" and achieves RT (%) of 100 across cases when using both spatial and structured features (Pre-RT (Both) = 84/78/85/83/80; RT (Both) = 100/100/100/100/100). Compared to the MCR baseline, FanoutNet shows different via and wire-length patterns: example #Via MCR = [133,164,307,262,337] vs FanoutNet = [113,185,393,302,359]; WL (10^2 mil) MCR = [174,199,480,740,464] vs FanoutNet = [170,228,520,772,477]. Commercial routers Freerouting and ELECTRA attain an average routability of ~99%; FreeRouting reduces wire length and via count but incurs a very long runtime, while ELECTRA "completes the routing in seconds for all test cases but produces higher wire length and via count." FanoutNet requires much longer training time, but inference (generating fanout results) occurs in seconds. Ablation shows fusing spatial and structured features improves pre-routability and final routing. Other methods: GA is slow and less effective; ARL improves quality efficiently; NB-A* prioritizes routability but struggles to balance multi-objective weights in complex cases.


---

## pqac-00000008

From text 'li2023fanoutnetaneuralized pages 1-2'.

Haiyun Li, Jixin Zhang, Ning Xu, and Mingyu Liu. Fanoutnet: a neuralized pcb fanout automation method using deep reinforcement learning. Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence, 37:8554-8561, Jun 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1609/aaai.v37i7.26030, doi:10.1609/aaai.v37i7.26030. This article has 15 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes FanoutNet, a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) method for automating PCB fanout. The authors formulate PCB fanout as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) and train policy and value models using representations that combine a structured feature sequence (pin/net info and partial fanout) and a rasterized spatial feature (multi-channel binary image). The encoder architecture uses CNNs plus an attention-based network; training and optimization use Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) iteratively. They integrate FanoutNet with a Multi-Constraint Router (MCR) to validate fanout under industrial routing constraints. On real-world industrial PCB benchmarks the paper reports that their approach achieves 100% routability in all industrial cases and reduces wire length by an average of 6.8% compared with prior methods. The excerpt does not report runtime, completion-rate, or component-placement-specific quantitative metrics, nor broader DRL-for-placement runtimes; those details are not present here.


---

## pqac-00000009

From text 'merrill2021hungryforfully pages 85-89'.

DJ Merrill. Hungry for fully automated design of embedded systems? Unknown journal, 2021.

The excerpt describes methods for automated PCB placement and routing used in Merrill (2021). Placement: a simulated annealing placer with a mixed objective penalizing HPWL, a routing-congestion metric, and component overlap (using Boost.Polygon). Annealing starts with 100% weight on HPWL and congestion and transitions to 100% overlap weight by the end; final allowed Manhattan move radius is 1 mm, rotation moves are fixed at 25%, and overlap is penalized with quadratic + linear + distance-based terms to discourage and resolve overlaps. Routing: a grid-based A* iterative rip-up-and-reroute router; designs are rasterized, nets routed to minimum cost, then all nets are ripped up and rerouted (one iteration); the reported experiments used 20 iterations. A partial cost update uses a rasterized circle of trace-width radius; large vias slow the algorithm while laser vias give ~4x speedup over through-hole vias. The autorouter favors layout-vs-schematic completion over strict DRC-clean routing; FreeRouting is DRV-clean and requires KiCad conversion. The framework is open-source on GitHub. The excerpt contains methods and implementation details but does not provide quantitative outcome metrics (wirelength improvement percentages, routability/completion rates, runtimes) nor data about FanoutNet or deep reinforcement-learning PCB approaches.

function.arguments: {"question": "What are the quantitative results for DREAMPlace GPU-accelerated placement, including wirelength reduction, runtime speedup over CPU placers, and benchmark results on ISPD or ICCAD testcases?"}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000010

From text 'rajarathnam2024enablingscalable& pages 56-62'.

RS Rajarathnam. Enabling scalable & secure open-source physical design for modern vlsi. Unknown journal, 2024.

The excerpt reports quantitative comparisons on ISPD'2016 benchmarks for GPU-accelerated placers. DREAMPlaceFPGA (implemented on a deep-learning toolkit) attains 1.08× faster global placer runtime than elfPlace-GPU and 4.77× faster than elfPlace-CPU. elfPlace-GPU itself achieves 4.4× speedup over a 20-thread elfPlace-CPU. However, both elfPlace-GPU and DREAMPlaceFPGA incur small HPWL degradations of 0.1% and 0.5% respectively versus elfPlace-CPU. IO overhead is significant for DREAMPlaceFPGA: IO accounts for ≈7% of total placement runtime (versus 1.7% and 3.1% for elfPlace-CPU and elfPlace-GPU), and the fixed intermediate DB (FIdB) is 28% faster than a CIdB but ≈2.2× slower in IO than elfPlace. Database format differences explain runtime gaps ("the 19% runtime difference for global placement"). Routing runtimes: elfPlace-GPU shows a 9% higher routing runtime than elfPlace-CPU; DREAMPlaceFPGA routing runtime is similar to elfPlace-GPU (≈1% higher) and ≈10% higher than elfPlace-CPU. For a congested case (FPGA05) DREAMPlaceFPGA produced placement causing a 20% higher Vivado routing runtime, though across 12 benchmarks DREAMPlaceFPGA had lower routing runtime on 8 of them. Vivado routing of FPGA05 took >1.5 hours for all placers.


---

## pqac-00000011

From text 'lin2020dreamplace2.0opensource pages 3-4'.

Yibo Lin, David Z. Pan, Haoxing Ren, and Brucek Khailany. Dreamplace 2.0: open-source gpu-accelerated global and detailed placement for large-scale vlsi designs. 2020 China Semiconductor Technology International Conference (CSTIC), pages 1-4, Jun 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1109/cstic49141.2020.9282573, doi:10.1109/cstic49141.2020.9282573. This article has 31 citations.

The excerpt reports quantitative results for DREAMPlace 2.0 on ISPD 2005 benchmarks. Table I (ISPD 2005 contest, float32) shows HPWL and runtime breakdowns per design; for example adaptec1 reports nearly identical HPWL (RePlAce GP 73.23 vs DREAMPlace GP 73.19) with total runtimes reduced (RePlAce total 118s vs DREAMPlace total 84s in the CPU 40-thread comparison). The paper states that DREAMPlace achieves a 1.3× speedup over RePlAce when using 40 CPU threads and achieves about 14× speedup when using the GPU (stated both as "14× speedup on GPU" and "more than 14× speedup over the state-of-the-art RePlAce using 40 CPU threads"). Solver comparisons note Nesterov gives similar solution quality to Adam but is ~2× faster in GP, while SGD with momentum yields about 1.2% worse wirelength than Nesterov/Adam. Overall, DREAMPlace matches or very closely matches RePlAce wirelength on ISPD testcases while substantially reducing runtime, especially with GPU acceleration.


---

## pqac-00000012

From text 'rajarathnam2024enablingscalable& pages 52-54'.

RS Rajarathnam. Enabling scalable & secure open-source physical design for modern vlsi. Unknown journal, 2024.

The excerpt reports DREAMPlaceFPGA evaluated on the ISPD’2016 contest benchmarks (12 synthetic FPGA designs). Table 2.2 gives per-design placement HPWL and routed wirelength (RWL) for elfPlace-CPU, elfPlace-GPU, and DREAMPlaceFPGA. The authors state that DREAMPlaceFPGA’s global placer "incurs 0.5% and 0.3% higher placement HPWL and routed wirelength than elfPlace-CPU across all the benchmarks due to the implementation differences." elfPlace-CPU and elfPlace-GPU produce very similar HPWL and RWL (elfPlace-GPU only accelerates some operators on GPU). Example per-design entries show close parity (e.g., FPGA01: CPU 273/318, GPU 274/317, DREAMPlaceFPGA 275/318; FPGA08: CPU 7,108/7,661 vs DREAMPlaceFPGA 6,884/7,475; FPGA11: CPU 9,450/10,421 vs DREAMPlaceFPGA 9,360/10,340). A "Geo. Ratio" row shows near-1.00 ratios across placers. The designs were routed with AMD/Xilinx Vivado v2015.4 to measure routability and RWL. The excerpt does not present a clear, attributable single-number runtime speedup of DREAMPlace (or DREAMPlaceFPGA) versus CPU placers—runtime data appears in the surrounding table but is not clearly labeled/parsable in this excerpt.


---

## pqac-00000013

From text 'lin2020dreamplace2.0opensource pages 2-3'.

## 1. crocker2021physicallyconstrainedpcb pages 41-49 (Context ID: pqac-00000015)

ion provides a proof of concept, validating

the methods in restricted domains, yet revealing limitations in scaling.


6.1  Do the methods described described by Mirho-

      seini and Goldie et al. show potential for physi-

      cally realized boards?


One of the largest challenges in RL is the design and execution of reward systems

that reflect the true intention of the researchers. While the heuristic rewards used by

Mirhoseini and Goldie et al. worked very well to generate boards that looked good to

the eye, they avoided the true intention of a PCB designer when using the proposed

technology: generate board placements that function and can be manufactured with

minimal tuning. Unfortunately, given the limits on current state-of-the-art routing

algorithms, non-heuristic reward systems for PCB placement may be years down the

line. Nevertheless, for the restricted ring placement case where non-heuristic reward


                                    41calculation is fast, this exploration produced a series of excellent ring placements,

showing the potential for these methods if heuristic rewards can be avoided.

  By modifying the methods outlined by Mirhoseini and Goldie et al.  to avoid

heuristic reward systems, this exploration produced a series of placements that not

only improved with training, but maintained physical validity. The success of the sys-

tem was largely due to ring placements, a narrow subset of general PCB placements.

The narrowing of the placement space to ring placements allowed for true routing to

be calculated after each placement step, a calculation that is computationally pro-

hibitive in the general case yet reasonable for ring structures. The two figures below

show two auto placements from the same trained model, and their corresponding

physical footprints. The two placements, one with 8 components and the other with

12, expose three key features about the placement model:

   1. The model places valid rings, ensuring all routing can be completed successfully.


   2. The model is not deterministic, and exhibits an exploration of placement con-

      figurations.


   3. The placements are physically verifiable. No components hang off of edges or

      overlap other components.

   The two placements below were trained with the following parameters:

                           Training Hyperparameters

                   Hyperparameter       Value

                      Discretization         10

                    Action Space          (10 × 10 × 2)

                      Policy Depth         5 TCNN Layers

                   Encoder Limit        10 Components

                   Entropy Bonus        0.005

                     Clipping 𝜖              0.2

                    Learning Rate      1𝑒−5

                    Episodes Run         2 Million



                                    42          Figure 6-1: AI Generated Placement With Large Components

In this AI generated placement, the agent successfully placed 4 components resulting
 in a board with 8 components total. The first AI placed component (red) was very
  large and sizes decayed roughly with each subsequent placements (anti-clockwise
around the ring). Importantly, the AI Agent considered rotation, and in many cases
   rotated components in order to prevent overlaps or overhangs. The second AI
   placed component (green) would overhang the canvas if not for it’s rotation.





                                    43            Figure 6-2: AI Generated Placement With 12 Components

    In this AI generated placement, the agent successfully placed 8 components
resulting in a board with 12 components total. Here, it is clear that the agent placed
 with less density in the bottom left of the board. The first few components placed
  are typically larger than the rest, and the agent leaves space to account for this.





                                    446.2  Method Limitations


While the modified methods proposed in this exploration show success in small ring

placements, they reveal natural limits to the system.

    First, pre-training an encoder network on a dataset of generated ring placements

limits the sizes of boards that this system can place. In this thesis, the encoder was

trained on up to 10 components, which resulted in a loss in performance beyond 12

component placements. This is due to the fact that the encoder is unable to extract

salient information from placements with more components than it was trained on.

   In order to alleviate the size limitations imposed by the encoder, the encoder could

be included as part of the RL agent’s policy as was done by Mirhoseini and Goldie et

al. However, due to implementation restrictions described in section 5.1.1 including

the encoder as part of the policy was not possible and it had to be kept separate.

   Second, physically verified placements put a level of strain on the RL process

that may be better solved through tuning and an ensemble of placement algorithms.

This strain is caused by the massive number of placement options each component

can have. For example, each placed component on a real PCB is defined by is its

location, rotation, mirror, scale, layer, shape and other use-case dependent attributes.

Each placement attribute adds massive complexity to the action space, and must all

be considered if placements will remain physically valid at all points in the process.

   For the sake of this exploration, a component was defined by only its width and

height, and a placement only by its location and rotation. With each board discretized

into a 10×10 grid, the action space expanded as a product of the subspaces, resulting

in 200 placement options at each point in the placement process. Considering a more

detailed board with a 100 × 100 location grid, 4 rotation options, 2 mirror options,

and 2 placement layers, the action space expands to 160, 000 possible placements for

each component. This space is significantly reduced, as was done by Mirhoseini and

Goldie et al., if post-placement tuning is employed to verify placements.

   The second limit revealed was that of dataset diversity and reward hacking with

learned reward systems. In RL, it is important to have fast and informative reward


                                    45systems that guide the algorithm toward the successful completion of its task. Reward

values are usually calculated millions of times throughout the training process and

therefore need to run in a matter of milliseconds.  In many cases, calculating true

reward is challenging; either it is too slow or the information is unavailable.  In

these cases, researchers often use proxies to approximate true reward: either heuristic

measurements or learned rewards.

   To use learned rewards as a means to speed up reward calculation, a neural network

is trained to learn true reward from a large dataset of examples. Under this approach,

thousands of placements are routed ahead of time using traditional methods, forming

a large dataset of placements and their true reward. A neural network is then trained

to predict routing success when given an unknown placement.  The hope of this

approach is that instead of calculating true reward at each step of the RL loop, the

neural network will predict true reward to great accuracy in a fraction of the time.

Unfortunately, this process is extremely vulnerable to reward hacking, a process where

an agent learns to maximize the network instead of the true reward the network

intends to emulate.

   To confirm this, for this exploration a network was pre-trained to predict  if a

placement would pass the ring condition to be used as a reward calculation. Using the

trained network as the reward for a successful placement, the original reinforcement

agent was again trained in an unbounded fashion. In a few million epochs the agent

was able to hack the learned reward system and formed placements that were invalid

rings, but received high reward.





                                    46                   Figure 6-3: Example of AI Reward Hacking

 To generate this placement, reward was calculated via a neural network trained to
  classify ring placements. This is an example of "Reward Hacking", where an RL
agent learns to maximize a reward function and fails at the intended task. Since the
     reward network was trained on a set distribution of ring placements, for
   out-of-distribution placements like the one above the reward function fails the
intended purpose and rewards placements highly even if they are clearly not rings.





                                    4748Chapter 7


Future Work




The logical next steps for the methods proposed in this exploration would be to

expand the design space into larger and more complex boards. While such a jump

seems small, this section will expose steps needed before it can be made. Through

means of constrained spaces 

## 2. huang2021rankingcostbuilding pages 7-9 (Context ID: pqac-00000016)

ormation. For example, when routing the ﬁrst net, its ranking index is j = 1 and it will use all the
rest k −1 cost maps. As a result, the ﬁrst routed path focuses more on its impact on other unrouted
nets. When routing the last net, its ranking index is j = k and no cost map will be used. As a result,
the last net is routed via a normal A* algorithm and the searched solution is the shortest path at
the current situation. As all the previous nets have been routed, the best way to route the last net
is just to ﬁnd out the shortest path. Our RC algorithm achieves this naturally and makes it ﬂexible
for arbitrary ranking orders. When the ranking order changes, the order and the usage of cost maps
change accordingly. In this way, the reward function can be deﬁned as:

                              F(θr, θc) = −L(Rθr, Cθc),                              (11)
and both θr and θc can be updated via the OpenAI-ES algorithm. Actually, the reward function F
can be integrated with arbitrary metrics, such as the signal latency. Thus the cost maps are free to
adapt to new constraints and design rules.

In the OpenAI-ES algorithm, we will sample a ﬁxed number of Gaussian noises and add them to the
original parameters to form new parameters. These new parameters are then fed into n evaluators
(or workers). The evaluator has an independent environment and executes the algorithm based on
received parameters. Finally, each evaluator will return a scalar reward. To stabilize the training
process, we normalize collected rewards {r1, ..., rn} from all the evaluators:

                                       ri = (ri −rmean)/rstd,                                (12)

where rmean and rstd are the mean and standard deviation of all the rewards.

To further improve the ﬁnal results, we add a post-processing step to the solution obtained from the
Ranking Cost algorithm. In the post-processing step, we will choose a connected path and re-plan it
using the canonical A* algorithm while keeping other paths ﬁxed. If we ﬁnd a shorter path during
the re-planning, we will update the current path to the new one. This post-processing step will be
iteratively applied on each path until all the paths can not be updated. Algorithm 1 shows the overall
training procedure of our RC algorithm. In practice, all the evaluators can execute in parallel as
proposed in (Salimans et al., 2017).

5  Experiments

In this section, we will compare our proposed method with some other baselines. We will also study
how ranking learning and cost maps work in our algorithm. Finally, we further show the scalability
of our algorithm for larger applications.

5.1  Methods Evaluation

To evaluate our algorithm, we build a grid map simulator as the test environment. We construct
300 maps with three different sizes: 16 × 16, 32 × 32 and 64 × 64. These maps are split into two
types, i.e., a simple one without obstacle and a more complex one with obstacles. Figure 1(a) and
Figure 1(b) show some of the maps used in our experiments. Algorithms will be evaluated on these
maps, and the success rates and the average of total lengths will be reported. Our experiments are
performed on a desktop machine with 128 GB RAM and one 64-core CPU. More details about the
maps can be found in the supplementary material.

Baselines:
Sequential A*: Sequential A* is a common used search-based algorithm in circuit routing. It routes
nets with a speciﬁc ranking order and different orders will lead to different results. In the experiments,
we randomly sample 5 different ranking orders and run the algorithm 5 times. The best score of 5
runs will be reported. To build a stronger baseline, we further randomly sample 200 different ranking
orders and the best score of 200 runs will be reported.

Value Iteration Networks (VINs) (Tamar et al., 2016): The VIN is a learning-based approach for
routing on grid maps. VINs use the value iteration from reinforcement learning and can achieve
better performance compared with supervised models. We apply VINs to the circuit routing problem
by sequentially executing it on each net. Similar to the sequential A* algorithm, we randomly sample
ﬁve different ranking orders and the best score of ﬁve runs will be reported.
Cost-Map Learning (CML): We construct a Cost-Map Learning algorithm from the Ranking Cost.
In the CML, the ranking parameter will not be trained (the ranking order is ﬁxed) and only the
cost-map will be trained.


The Ranking Cost as presented in Algorithm 1 is our ﬁnal method for circuit routing problem

From text 'lai2023chipformertransferablechip pages 15-17'.

The provided excerpt reports quantitative results for ChiPFormer (called ChiPFormer(workflow) in Table 8) on mixed-size placement (ICCAD04 benchmark). Table 8 lists HPWL (×10^7) per circuit; example values include ibm01: 16.70 (×10^7) with a reported -26.82% (relative improvement shown in table), ibm10: 230.39 (×10^7) (-16.00%), and ibm18: 370.36 (×10^7) (-7.14%). The paper states ChiPFormer(workflow) "can achieve the best placement quality." The excerpt does not contain any quantitative results for "chip placement with diffusion," "DeepTH three-head chip placement," or "Bayesian optimization for macro placement," so no HPWL, runtime, or comparison metrics for those methods are available in these pages.

function.arguments: {"question": "What are the quantitative results and key findings for global placement with deep learning routability optimization (Liu et al. 2021), DRC-aware routing, and neural network fitness function for PCB design automation (Murphy 2020)? Include congestion reduction, wirelength, DRC violations.", "text_name_focus_list": ["liu2021globalplacementwith pages 3-4", "liu2021globalplacementwith pages 4-4", "murphy2020neuralnetworkfitness pages 5-11", "murphy2020neuralnetworkfitness pages 36-40"]}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000020

From text 'murphy2020neuralnetworkfitness pages 36-40'.

JR Murphy. Neural network fitness function for optimization-based approaches to pcb design automation. Unknown journal, 2020.

Murphy (2020) evaluates neural-network-based routability predictors against simple metrics (HPWL, crossings) and reports substantially lower relative-routability error rates for the learned models. For single-board experiments (Boards 1 and 2) the learned models outperform HPWL and crossings; error rates fall rapidly as the routability-difference between compared placements increases (for Board 1 the error rate reaches zero once the routability difference exceeds 0.04). Board 2 is harder: routability differences must exceed 0.08 for error rates to drop below 1%. In multiboard (no novel board) training, the network predicting absolute routability attains MAE 0.0182 on the combined test set (per-board MAEs: Board1 0.0112, Board2 0.0235, Board3 0.013, Board4 0.0318, Board5 0.0118). For the novel-board experiment (Board 6), the multiboard-trained model has very high absolute MAE on Board 6 (0.2205) but a relative-routability error of 0.1838, indicating it learned features useful for ranking placements. Fine-tuning with small Board 6 datasets greatly reduces absolute MAE (e.g., to ~0.0128/0.0123 with 25/50 examples) while relative error remains around 0.18–0.19, with modest improvements as more examples or layer freezing are used.


---

## pqac-00000021

From text 'liu2021globalplacementwith pages 4-4'.

Siting Liu, Qi Sun, Peiyu Liao, Yibo Lin, and Bei Yu. Global placement with deep learning-enabled explicit routability optimization. 2021 Design, Automation & Test in Europe Conference & Exhibition (DATE), pages 1821-1824, Feb 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.23919/date51398.2021.9473959, doi:10.23919/date51398.2021.9473959. This article has 79 citations.

Liu et al. (2021) develop a routability-driven placer using a deep learning model (fully convolutional networks) integrated into the placement objective to explicitly guide cell movement. Evaluation on modified ISPD2015 benchmarks shows up to 9.05% reduction in congestion rate (reported versus DREAMPlace) and a reported 18.68% reduction versus NTUplace4dr in one statement; routed wirelength is reduced by 5.30% compared with DREAMPlace and NTUplace4dr. The method provides efficient and relatively accurate routing congestion modeling at placement. Runtime: average runtime is about 4× slower than DREAMPlace but roughly 23× faster than NTUplace4dr due to native GPU acceleration. Runtime breakdown (Fig. 5) highlights that computing congestion penalty gradients ∇L is the most time-consuming step (46.10% of total runtime), computing L (features extraction, inference, l2-norm) takes 17.23%, and the l2-norm operator in PyTorch alone accounts for 13.6% and is noted as optimizable. The approach handles non-differentiability by discrete computation of some components.

## pqac-00000022

From text 'cheng2023assessmentofreinforcement pages 10-11'.

Chung-Kuan Cheng, Andrew B. Kahng, Sayak Kundu, Yucheng Wang, and Zhiang Wang. Assessment of reinforcement learning for macro placement. Proceedings of the 2023 International Symposium on Physical Design, pages 158-166, Mar 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3569052.3578926, doi:10.1145/3569052.3578926. This article has 70 citations.

Cheng et al. (ISPD 2023) provide quantitative comparisons between Circuit Training (CT, RL-based) and other placers. They report final-proxy-cost numbers for one run: proxy wirelength = 0.0913, proxy density = 0.5290, proxy congestion = 0.9017. Overall, human experts and simulated annealing (SA) often outperform the RL framework: on ICCAD04 benchmarks SA beats CT on proxy cost in all cases and on HPWL in 16 of 17 cases. On modern benchmarks, SA beats CT on proxy cost in 4 of 5 cases and on routed wirelength in 5 of 6 cases. CT narrowly outperforms RePlAce on modern testcases for proxy cost and the Nature Table 1 metrics when CT uses an initial physical-synthesis placement; in 6 head-to-head comparisons RePlAce still wins 3/6 routed wirelength comparisons and 2/6 total power comparisons. Against prior “Stronger Baselines” results, Cheng et al. confirm SA outperforms CT on ICCAD04 (CT wins only 1/17 HPWL comparisons) and note that RePlAce beats CT on ICCAD04 where CT did not win any proxy cost or HPWL comparisons. They also note pre-training effects: the Nature paper showed pre-training benefits mainly for runtime and final proxy cost, and Cheng et al. used eight NVIDIA V100 GPUs with global batch size 1024 to reproduce CT runs.


---

## pqac-00000023

From text 'cheng2023assessmentofreinforcement pages 9-10'.

Chung-Kuan Cheng, Andrew B. Kahng, Sayak Kundu, Yucheng Wang, and Zhiang Wang. Assessment of reinforcement learning for macro placement. Proceedings of the 2023 International Symposium on Physical Design, pages 158-166, Mar 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3569052.3578926, doi:10.1145/3569052.3578926. This article has 70 citations.

The excerpt reports quantitative runtime and some proxy-cost results from Cheng et al. (ISPD 2023) comparing Circuit Training (CT) to CMP, RePlAce, simulated annealing (SA) and AutoDMP on three NG45 enablements. Table 8 shows CT training runtimes: Ariane-NG45 32.31 hours, BlackParrot-NG45 50.51 hours, and MemPool-NG45 81.23 hours. By contrast, CMP and RePlAce complete in minutes to a few hours (e.g., Ariane: CMP 0.05 h, RePlAce 0.06 h; BlackParrot: CMP 0.33 h, RePlAce 2.52 h; MemPool: CMP 1.97 h, RePlAce N.A.), SA was run with a budgeted stop of 12.5 hours per run, and AutoDMP runtimes were modest (Ariane 0.29 h, BlackParrot 0.71 h, MemPool 1.73 h). The paper notes CT uses substantially more compute: CT training used eight NVIDIA V100 GPUs plus two collect servers (each with 96 CPUs running 13 collect jobs). SA was run as 320 parallel jobs (1 CPU thread each). The excerpt also reports proxy costs for the final CT solution on Ariane: proxy wirelength 0.0913, proxy density 0.5290, and proxy congestion 0.9017. The document states CT consumes substantially more runtime/compute than other placers. The excerpt does not include explicit HPWL/post-route wirelength numeric comparisons across placers or detailed benchmark HPWL deltas.

## pqac-00000024

From text 'cheng2025anupdatedassessment pages 2-3'.

Chung-Kuan Cheng, Andrew B. Kahng, Sayak Kundu, Yucheng Wang, and Zhiang Wang. An updated assessment of reinforcement learning for macro placement. IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems, pages 1-1, Jan 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1109/tcad.2025.3644293, doi:10.1109/tcad.2025.3644293. This article has 45 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The provided excerpt documents methodology and comparative setup from Cheng et al. (updated assessment). It describes Circuit Training (CT) as an RL method that sequentially places macros and defines the proxy cost used for RL reward as composed of wirelength, density, and congestion. The paper evaluates three CT variants: CT-Scratch (train from scratch), CT-AC (fine-tune Google’s released checkpoint), and CT-Ours (fine-tune the authors' own pre-trained checkpoint). Classical placers used for comparison include an improved simulated-annealing (SA) baseline (multi-threaded, go-with-the-winners enhancements), RePlAce (electrostatic model from OpenROAD), Cadence CMP (commercial placer), and human-expert placements. The authors report that their improved SA can yield up to 26% better proxy cost within the same runtime while using only one-quarter of the resources versus a prior SA implementation, and that SA and human baselines remain superior to the latest AlphaChip while using substantially fewer resources. They also doubled CT iterations from 200 to 400 to aid convergence and added sub-10nm testcase enablements. The excerpt does not include detailed per-benchmark numerical tables for HPWL, routed wirelength, runtimes, or explicit benchmark-by-benchmark comparisons.

## pqac-00000025

From text 'cheng2025anupdatedassessment pages 12-13'.

Chung-Kuan Cheng, Andrew B. Kahng, Sayak Kundu, Yucheng Wang, and Zhiang Wang. An updated assessment of reinforcement learning for macro placement. IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems, pages 1-1, Jan 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1109/tcad.2025.3644293, doi:10.1109/tcad.2025.3644293. This article has 45 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports qualitative and some quantitative comparisons in the updated assessment of Circuit Training (AlphaChip) versus classical baselines. Key numeric details: a pre-trained CT-AC model used 20 diverse TPU blocks; CT-Ariane-X4 is a large testcase with 532 macros (TSMC 7nm). Pre-training behavior: the model pre-trained on other slices shows a sharp placement-return jump at 50 hours but only ~4% further improvement over the next 125 hours. Table XI and Fig. 6 (discussed) link pre-training dataset diversity and number of macros to convergence (examples show Max #Macro values like 324 and 133 across slices). The authors trained AlphaChip from scratch and fine-tuned the released checkpoint, and they strengthened the simulated annealing (SA) baseline (multi-threading + a 1994 “go-with-the-winners” metaheuristic). Their experiments report that SA achieves better results than AlphaChip on CT-Ariane-X4 in both post-detailed placement HPWL and CT proxy cost while using a fraction of the runtime and compute. They also note that the CT proxy objective correlates weakly with final post-route PPA metrics. The excerpt does not provide explicit numeric HPWL, wirelength, runtime, or full benchmark tables comparing Circuit Training, RePlAce, and other placers.

## pqac-00000026

From text 'cheng2021onjointlearning pages 7-9'.

Ruoyu Cheng and Junchi Yan. On joint learning for solving placement and routing in chip design. Preprint, Jan 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2111.00234, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2111.00234. This article has 163 citations.

The excerpt reports quantitative results for the authors' RL-based macro placement (DeepPlace) on ISPD-2005 circuits and compares it to a sequential learning placer and DREAMPlace/RePlace. Table 2 lists total wirelength (presumably HPWL or routing wirelength) for several circuits: adaptec1 — DeepPlace 80,117,232 vs Sequential 86,675,688 vs DREAMPlace 83,749,616; adaptec3 — DeepPlace 241,072,304 vs Sequential 257,956,784 vs DREAMPlace 264,789,408; bigblue1 — DeepPlace 140,435,296 vs Sequential 168,456,768 (DREAMPlace: congestion check failure); bigblue3 — DeepPlace 450,633,360 vs Sequential 460,581,568 (DREAMPlace: congestion check failure); bigblue4 — DeepPlace 951,984,128 vs Sequential 1,039,976,064 (DREAMPlace: congestion check failure). The authors note DREAMPlace often fails congestion checks (likely due to macro overlap) while DeepPlace and the sequential RL placer produce viable placements. Runtime: the joint method achieves approximately a 4× speedup over RePlace in total runtime. Pretraining helps: pretraining the policy on a macro-only netlist for ~300 iterations produced the best results (500 iterations led to overfitting and worse final placement). For joint placement+routing (DeepPR) the RL agent significantly improves final routed wirelength compared with a separate placement-then-routing pipeline, and the authors argue HPWL is not always an accurate proxy for routed wirelength. The excerpt does not contain comparisons to simulated annealing or a 2023 assessment; it focuses on the 2021 DeepPlace/DeepPR results and runtime comparisons to RePlace/DREAMPlace.

function.arguments: {"question": "What does Murphy 2020 report about neural network fitness function for PCB design automation? What is the methodology, datasets, and key conclusions about using neural networks to predict routability for PCB component placement optimization?", "text_name_focus_list": ["murphy2020neuralnetworkfitness pages 1-5", "murphy2020neuralnetworkfitness pages 5-11", "murphy2020neuralnetworkfitness pages 40-48"]}

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000027

From text 'murphy2020neuralnetworkfitness pages 1-5'.

JR Murphy. Neural network fitness function for optimization-based approaches to pcb design automation. Unknown journal, 2020.

Murphy (2020) develops a neural-network–based fitness function to predict routability of PCB placements so it can be used as the scoring function in iterative-improvement placement optimizers. Routability is defined as the completion rate a placement would achieve when routed. The work generates a labeled dataset of over 75,000 placed PCB designs derived from six different boards (data generation used AWS cloud resources). Labels include routability measurements (the thesis includes routability-assignment variance tests where each design was routed 100 times). The experimental pipeline explores baseline feature representations and CNN-based image features (including experiments with VGG16), and evaluates models on single-board and multi-board prediction tasks. Models are assessed for both absolute routability prediction and relative routability ranking. The thesis reports that the neural network predictors provide improvement over traditional routability proxies (e.g., wire length and connection-crossing estimates), demonstrating feasibility of using learned routability estimates as placement scoring functions for optimization-based PCB placement automation.


---

## pqac-00000028

From text 'murphy2020neuralnetworkfitnessa pages 16-20'.

JR Murphy. Neural network fitness function for optimization-based approaches to pcb design automation. Unknown journal, 2020.

Murphy (2020) argues that common heuristics used as placement fitness functions (e.g., normalized crossings, HPWL) do not correlate strongly enough with true routability: routability spans large ranges for fixed heuristic values, and this spread is much larger than the measured stochastic routing variance (Board 1 SD ≈ 0.0053, Board 2 SD ≈ 0.0111 from 5 designs routed 100 times). To improve fitness evaluation, Murphy trained a deep neural network to predict routability from a placed PCB representation using supervised learning. The training dataset comprises placements from six boards (three small <100 components, three large >300 components). Because the GO-based auto-placer used to generate placements explored only a subset of the placement space, a data augmenter was implemented that applies translations, rotations, and swaps to broaden coverage; roughly half of placements are augmented. Dataset sizes per board are reported (training sets: e.g., Board1 12,136; Board4 20,878; test sets listed as well). Murphy expects the network to learn nuanced latent features that better match true routability than traditional heuristics, though the dataset of six boards is acknowledged as limited and future work will expand it.


---

## pqac-00000029

From text 'murphy2020neuralnetworkfitnessa pages 1-5'.

JR Murphy. Neural network fitness function for optimization-based approaches to pcb design automation. Unknown journal, 2020.

Murphy (2020) addresses automating PCB placement by learning a placement scoring function that correlates with routability (the fraction of net connections that can be routed without rule violations). The thesis focuses on iterative-improvement placement algorithms, where scoring heavily influences optimization. Existing proxies (wire length, estimated crossings) correlate only roughly with routability; Murphy develops a neural network to predict routability from placed designs to serve as a fitness function. A dataset of over 75,000 placed PCB designs derived from six different PCBs was generated (data-generation pipeline and AWS cloud resources are documented). Experiments cover single-board and multi-board prediction tasks; networks are trained/evaluated to predict both absolute routability and relative routability (ranking placements). The work includes baseline feature representations and CNN architectures (and image-feature experiments including VGG16), plus routability-variance tests (each design routed 100 times). The reported outcome is that the neural-network predictor improves over traditional routability proxies, demonstrating feasibility of using learned routability estimates as placement scoring functions for optimization-based PCB design automation.


---

## pqac-00000030

From text 'murphy2020neuralnetworkfitness pages 40-48'.

JR Murphy. Neural network fitness function for optimization-based approaches to pcb design automation. Unknown journal, 2020.

Murphy (2020) trains convolutional neural networks to predict PCB routability and tests their use as fitness functions for placement optimization. Methodology: networks were trained on multi-board data (Boards 1–5) and then three variants were ﬁnetuned on a novel PCB (Board 6) using small sets of 25 and 50 Board 6 examples: (1) the pretrained model, (2) the pretrained model with convolutional layers frozen, and (3) the same architecture with randomly initialized weights. Evaluation used absolute routability (MAE) and relative routability (ranking/classification) tasks. Datasets: Table 9 lists dataset sizes for Boards 1–6 (test set for Board 6 = 1,663). Key findings: finetuning dramatically reduced absolute MAE on Board 6 (original MAE 0.2205 -> 0.0123 with 25 points), but provided only minor gains for relative routability. Because placement algorithms (e.g., GO) rely on relative scoring, improved absolute calibration does not necessarily translate to better placement outcomes. A randomly initialized model ﬁnetuned on few Board 6 points matched the relative performance of models trained on Boards 1–5. Future work recommended larger/more varied datasets, integration into placement algorithms, and improving relative routability prediction and non-image features.

## pqac-00000031

From text 'murphy2020neuralnetworkfitness pages 16-20'.

JR Murphy. Neural network fitness function for optimization-based approaches to pcb design automation. Unknown journal, 2020.

Murphy (2020) argues that standard heuristics (e.g., HPWL, crossings) only partially correlate with true PCB placement routability and have "room for improvement." To address this, he trains a supervised deep neural network that takes a placed-PCB representation as input and predicts a routability score, with the aim of learning latent features more predictive of routability than simple heuristics. Data were generated primarily from a GO-based auto-placer across six boards (three small <100 components and three complex >300 components). Because the auto-placer explored only a limited portion of placement space, Murphy augmented generated placements by applying tunable translations, rotations, and swaps; roughly half the dataset is augmented. Routability labels come from routing runs; variance analysis (100 routings per design) found mean standard deviations of routability around 0.0053 (Board 1) and 0.0111 (Board 2), while heuristic-conditioned routability ranges (e.g., 0.83–0.94 at a fixed crossings value) are much larger, indicating heuristics cannot explain the spread. Dataset sizes are reported in Table 2. Murphy concludes a neural-network fitness function can better match true routability and improve placement optimization, but notes the dataset is limited and should be expanded in future work.

function.arguments:
{"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a comprehensive summary table of ~13-15 papers on ML/RL/optimization-based methods for PCB and chip physical design. The table should have columns: Reference (short citation), Category (e.g. Chip Placement, PCB Placement, Routing, DRC-Aware, Benchmark/Survey), Method Type (e.g. Deep RL, Offline RL, GPU-Accelerated Analytical, Evolution-based, NN Fitness, SA+RL), Key Quantitative Results (wirelength, congestion, runtime improvements), and Known Limitations. 

Papers to include:
1. Mirhoseini & Goldie, Nature 2021 - RL chip placement
2. Cheng et al., ISPD 2023 - Assessment of RL for macro placement
3. Cheng et al., IEEE TCAD 2025 - Updated assessment
4. Lai et al., ChiPFormer 2023 - Offline decision transformer
5. Cheng & Yan, DeepPlace/DeepPR 2021 - Joint placement+routing RL
6. Lin et al., DREAMPlace 2.0, CSTIC 2020 - GPU-accelerated placement
7. Liu et al., DATE 2021 - Routability-driven placement
8. Oh et al., 2022 - Bayesian optimization macro placement  
9. Crocker, 2021 - PCB placement with deep RL
10. Murphy, 2020 - NN fitness for PCB placement
11. Li et al., FanoutNet, AAAI 2023 - PCB fanout automation
12. Liao et al., JMD 2020 - DRL global routing
13. Huang et al., Ranking Cost 2021 - Evolution-based routing
14. Merrill, 2021 - Full PCB design automation"}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-00)

| Reference | Category | Method Type | Key Quantitative Results | Known Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mirhoseini et al., *Nature* 2021 | Chip Placement | Deep RL / graph policy | Reported as achieving placements comparable or superior to human floorplans in under 6 hours; widely cited as the first high-profile end-to-end RL chip macro placement result, but exact quantitative values were not available in the retrieved evidence here. Later assessments indicate its proxy objective emphasized wirelength, density, and congestion rather than full post-route manufacturability. | Reproducibility controversy; code/data initially incomplete; later critiques questioned dependence on pretraining, convergence, proxy-vs-final-PPA mismatch, and compute requirements (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000024) |
| Cheng et al., ISPD 2023 | Chip Placement / Benchmarking | Reproduction + stronger baselines (SA, RePlAce, CMP) | CT training runtimes: 32.31 h (Ariane-NG45), 50.51 h (BlackParrot-NG45), 81.23 h (MemPool-NG45). CMP/RePlAce ran in minutes to a few hours; SA beat CT on proxy cost in all ICCAD04 cases and on HPWL in 16/17 cases; on modern benchmarks SA beat CT on proxy cost in 4/5 and on routed wirelength in 5/6 cases. RePlAce won 3/6 routed-wirelength comparisons and 2/6 total-power comparisons in head-to-heads. Ariane final CT proxy example: WL 0.0913, density 0.5290, congestion 0.9017 (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023) | CT consumed far more compute than classical baselines; benefits depend on pretraining and initial placements; proxy-cost wins did not consistently translate to routed QoR wins (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023) |
| Cheng et al., *IEEE TCAD* 2025 | Chip Placement / Critique | Updated assessment; SA+RL comparison | Improved SA baseline achieved up to 26% better proxy cost within the same runtime while using one-quarter of the resources versus prior SA. On CT-Ariane-X4 (532 macros, TSMC 7nm), SA beat AlphaChip in both post-detailed-placement HPWL and CT proxy cost. Pretrained CT showed a large return jump by ~50 h but only ~4% improvement over the next 125 h (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000000) | Weak correlation between CT proxy and final post-route PPA; instability and dataset-diversity sensitivity in pretraining; no independent full reproduction of original Nature-level claims noted in the evidence (pqac-00000025, pqac-00000000) |
| Lai et al., ChiPFormer 2023 | Chip Placement | Offline RL / decision transformer | Claimed 10× runtime reduction and strong transfer to unseen circuits in the abstract. On ICCAD04 mixed-size placement, example HPWLs included ibm01 16.70×10^7, ibm10 230.39×10^7, ibm18 370.36×10^7, with tabled relative improvements such as -26.82%, -16.00%, and -7.14%; overlap was often 0.00% on many macro-placement cases. Evaluated on two RTX3090 GPUs (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000002) | Runtime curves shown, but explicit wall-clock numbers were not in retrieved evidence. As an offline-learning method, quality depends on offline data and subsequent finetuning; still relies on downstream legalizers/placers for full flow closure (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000002) |
| Cheng & Yan, DeepPlace / DeepPR 2021 | Chip Placement + Routing | Deep RL + GNN/CNN + DREAMPlace | DeepPlace beat sequential RL and often DREAMPlace on total wirelength: e.g., adaptec1 80,117,232 vs 86,675,688 (sequential) vs 83,749,616 (DREAMPlace); bigblue4 951,984,128 vs 1,039,976,064. DeepPR also improved final routed wirelength over a separate placement-then-routing pipeline; example benchmark pairs included 5298 vs 6077 and 63,560 vs 79,573. Reported ~4× total-runtime speedup over RePlAce (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000026) | Moderate macro-count scalability only; overfitting after too much pretraining; macro size/shape and richer human constraints not fully modeled; DREAMPlace baseline sometimes failed congestion checks due to macro overlap assumptions (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000004) |
| Lin et al., DREAMPlace 2.0 2020 | Chip Placement | GPU-accelerated analytical placement | Matched RePlAce wirelength closely while cutting runtime: adaptec1 total runtime 84 s vs 118 s for RePlAce in CPU comparison; overall 1.3× speedup over RePlAce on 40 CPU threads and ~14× speedup with GPU. Nesterov optimizer delivered similar quality to Adam but ~2× faster in GP; SGD with momentum was ~1.2% worse in wirelength (pqac-00000011, pqac-00000013) | Primarily wirelength-oriented analytical placement, not a full manufacturability-aware PCB solution by itself; routability/DRC must be handled by downstream stages or extensions (pqac-00000011, pqac-00000013) |
| Liu et al., DATE 2021 | DRC-/Routability-Aware Placement | Deep-learning congestion predictor integrated into placer | On modified ISPD2015, achieved up to 9.05% congestion-rate reduction and 5.30% routed-wirelength reduction versus DREAMPlace; one statement reports 18.68% congestion reduction versus NTUplace4dr. Runtime was ~4× slower than DREAMPlace but ~23× faster than NTUplace4dr; gradient computation for congestion penalty consumed 46.10% of runtime (pqac-00000021) | Not a full PCB-specific method; adds runtime overhead versus plain DREAMPlace; some components of the routability term are non-differentiable/discretized (pqac-00000021) |
| Oh et al., 2022 | Chip Placement | Bayesian optimization | Proposed Bayesian optimization over sequence pairs for fixed-outline macro placement and argued better sample efficiency than RL for expensive objectives. The retrieved evidence confirms competitive performance claims but did not provide usable numeric HPWL/runtime tables. | Evidence retrieved here lacked benchmark numbers; practical scalability for very large mixed-size industrial blocks remains less documented in the available excerpts (pqac-00000002) |
| Crocker, 2021 | PCB Placement | Deep RL with physical constraints | Demonstrated physically valid PCB ring placements after 2 million episodes using a 10×10×2 action space and 5-layer TCNN policy. In example outputs, the agent successfully generated manufacturable 8- and 12-component ring boards with no overlap or edge overhangs and complete routability for the restricted ring domain (pqac-00000015) | Proof-of-concept only for restricted ring placements; encoder trained up to 10 components degraded beyond ~12 components; true-routing reward is computationally prohibitive in general PCB placement; learned rewards showed reward hacking (pqac-00000015) |
| Murphy, 2020 | PCB Placement | NN fitness / routability predictor | Built a dataset of >75,000 placed PCB designs from six boards and showed neural predictors beat HPWL/crossing heuristics for routability estimation. Multi-board absolute-routability model reached MAE 0.0182 overall; per-board MAEs included 0.0112, 0.0235, 0.0130, 0.0318, 0.0118. For a novel board, MAE was initially 0.2205 but fine-tuning with 25–50 examples reduced it to ~0.0128/0.0123 (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000027, pqac-00000030) | Relative ranking on novel boards improved less than absolute calibration; only six boards in the dataset; still a surrogate fitness, not a full placer/router or guaranteed DRC-clean flow (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031) |
| Li et al., FanoutNet 2023 | PCB Routing / Fanout | Deep RL (PPO) + multi-constraint router | Achieved 100% routability on all listed open-source and industrial PCB cases. Open-source averages: 25 vias, 1,160 mm wirelength, 50 s runtime; industrial averages: 270 vias, WL 433 (table units), 245 min runtime. The paper states average wirelength improved by 6.8% over prior methods and inference ran in seconds after training (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008) | Focuses on fanout/pre-routing, not full end-to-end PCB autorouting; training cost is substantial; some cases increased via count or WL relative to one baseline despite better final routability (pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007) |
| Liao et al., JMD 2020 | Routing (PCB/IC global routing) | Deep RL / DQN | On 40 test routing problems, DQN achieved lower wirelength than A* in 80% (γ=1), 90% (γ=0.95), 97.5% (γ=0.9), and 95% (γ=0.8) of cases; with γ=0.9, all 40 problems had non-negative WL improvement over A*. DQN solutions had zero overflow in all cases, while A* occasionally had positive overflow; A*-based burn-in memory improved results, boosting better-than-A* cases from 57.5% to 80% (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018) | Mostly tested on generated grid-routing problems rather than full manufacturable PCB boards; sequential two-pin formulation limits direct extension to richer multi-pin industrial routing; scaling and dataset availability remain open issues (pqac-00000018) |
| Huang et al., Ranking Cost 2021 | Routing | Evolution-based optimization + learned ranking/cost maps | On 64×64 maps with obstacles, success rate improved to 0.32 with average length 358.5 versus Seq A*(200) at 0.26 and 367.5; on 16×16 and 32×32 maps, Ranking Cost often reached 1.0 success. Runtime was 38.3 s/map versus 41.5 s/map for Seq A*(200) and 0.8 s/map for Seq A*(5), trading more search for better solutions (pqac-00000016) | Grid-map benchmark abstraction, not direct fab-ready PCB routing; per-task learning adds overhead; more evidence needed on real PCB DRC/manufacturability constraints (pqac-00000016) |
| Merrill, 2021 | PCB Placement + Routing | Classical optimization (SA placer + A* rip-up/reroute) | Implemented a simulated-annealing placer with HPWL, congestion, and overlap terms plus a grid-based A* iterative rip-up-and-reroute router run for 20 iterations. Reported qualitative implementation facts include ~4× speedup when using laser vias over through-hole vias in routing experiments (pqac-00000009) | Prioritized completion over strict DRC-clean routing; evidence retrieved did not include systematic wirelength/routability benchmark tables; more of a prototype flow than a peer-reviewed industrial-grade PCB CAD benchmark paper (pqac-00000009) |


*Table: This table summarizes 14 key papers on ML, RL, and optimization methods for chip and PCB physical design, emphasizing quantitative results, method type, and practical limitations. It is useful for quickly comparing which approaches are most relevant to manufacturable PCB generation versus chip-oriented placement research.*

Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer: [artifact-00]

Context IDs that may be cited: [pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000019, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027, pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033]

Status: Paper Count=56 | Relevant Papers=14 | Clinical Trial Count=0 | Relevant Clinical Trials=0 | Current Evidence=34 | Current Cost=$0.2886 | Disease-Target Evidence=0
