contexts.context: The excerpt describes evaluation methods and quantitative experiments used to assess placement routability proxies. For two PCBs (one small, one large — the former ≈80 components, the latter ≈350) the authors generated just over 15,000 placed designs per board using their auto-placer. They then applied a via-placement engine to assign vias and layer-wise connections and routed each placement with a state-of-the-art autorouter; the resulting completion rate (completion of routed connections) was used as the "true routability" score. Because full routing during iterative placement is too slow, the work studies fast heuristics as fitness functions: HPWL, crossings, RUDY, and weighted combinations. They computed normalized HPWL and normalized crossings and report strong negative correlations with true routability: HPWL vs routability = -0.923 (Board 1) and -0.6979 (Board 2); normalized crossings vs routability = -0.898 (Board 1) and -0.8 (Board 2). The authors note substantial variance: e.g., at normalized crossings ≈0.075 routability ranged ≈0.83–0.94, illustrating that even well-scoring heuristic values can correspond to a wide range of true routabilities. The via-placement engine is GO-based and introduces randomness.

contexts.context: This excerpt describes DeepPlace/DeepPR, a reinforcement-learning-based joint macro/standard-cell placement and placement+routing approach for IC design (ISPD-2005 benchmarks). The implementation uses PPO (PyTorch), a 2-layer GCN backbone (32 and 16 channels), Adam with learning rate 2.5×10^-4, and experiments run on a single NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2080Ti. The authors use a pretraining curriculum on a partial netlist containing macros only; pretraining for 300 iterations gave the best downstream performance while excessive pretraining hurt exploration. Table results show DeepPlace achieves lower total wirelength than a sequential learning placer and DREAMPlace on tested circuits (example: adaptec1: DeepPlace 80,117,232 vs sequential 86,675,688 vs DREAMPlace 83,749,616; bigblue4: DeepPlace 951,984,128 vs sequential 1,039,976,064). DREAMPlace often fails congestion checks (overlap). The RL module is a small fraction of runtime and the total runtime of the proposed method is approximately 4× faster than RePlace. For joint placement+routing (DeepPR), the RL agent “dramatically improves” final routed wirelength versus a separate pipeline, indicating HPWL is an imperfect proxy for routed wirelength. Note: these results concern IC macro/standard-cell placement and routing, not PCB-specific tasks like component placement, FanoutNet, or PCB routing benchmarks.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes FanoutNet’s reinforcement-learning based fanout method combined with a Multi-Constraint Router (MCR). The network uses attention layers (MHA + FF) and an MLP policy/value head trained with PPO (K epochs = 20; learning rates 3e-4 and 1e-3). The MCR frames routing as a constrained 3-D A* search with cost ˆf(n)=ˆg(n)+ˆh(n); sub-costs include wire-length w(n)=∥nc−n∥2, layer-change cost v(n)=|z(nc)−z(n)|, and additional terms for differential pairs, reference-layer shielding, and spacing. The combined ˆg uses coefficients c1..c5 adjusted during iterations. Experiments use Python/PyTorch for RL and C++ (mingw64) for routing on an AMD Ryzen 7 5800X workstation. Benchmarks include 11 open-source and 5 industrial PCB cases. Evaluation metrics reported are Routability (RT), Via Count, Wire Length (WL), Runtime (T), and Pre-Routability for training. Comparative methods listed are Freerouting2, ELECTRA, NB-A*, GA, and ARL. The excerpt contains one quantitative routability fragment for MCR across cases: "MCR 100 85 98 99 97 RT (%)" but does not provide full, detailed numeric comparisons (wirelength improvements, completion rates, or runtime figures) between FanoutNet/deep-RL and the other methods within the provided text.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes methods for automated PCB placement and routing used in Merrill (2021). Placement: a simulated annealing placer with a mixed objective penalizing HPWL, a routing-congestion metric, and component overlap (using Boost.Polygon). Annealing starts with 100% weight on HPWL and congestion and transitions to 100% overlap weight by the end; final allowed Manhattan move radius is 1 mm, rotation moves are fixed at 25%, and overlap is penalized with quadratic + linear + distance-based terms to discourage and resolve overlaps. Routing: a grid-based A* iterative rip-up-and-reroute router; designs are rasterized, nets routed to minimum cost, then all nets are ripped up and rerouted (one iteration); the reported experiments used 20 iterations. A partial cost update uses a rasterized circle of trace-width radius; large vias slow the algorithm while laser vias give ~4x speedup over through-hole vias. The autorouter favors layout-vs-schematic completion over strict DRC-clean routing; FreeRouting is DRV-clean and requires KiCad conversion. The framework is open-source on GitHub. The excerpt contains methods and implementation details but does not provide quantitative outcome metrics (wirelength improvement percentages, routability/completion rates, runtimes) nor data about FanoutNet or deep reinforcement-learning PCB approaches.
contexts.question: What are the quantitative results and methods for PCB component placement automation, PCB routing, FanoutNet, and deep reinforcement learning approaches for PCB design? Include wirelength improvement, routability, completion rate, and runtime metrics.

snippets: We use a simulated annealing (SA) algorithm to ﬁnd a placement that is optimized with regards to the sum of an overlap penalization term, unrouted air-wire lengths, and a routing congestion prediction metric[SJ07].
Our objective function penalizes the sum of half-perimeter wire length (HPWL) over all nets, a routing congestion metric[SJ07], and a component overlap score.
At the beginning of annealing, the objective function places 100% of weight on HPWL and congestion and none on overlap.
Annealing ends with overlap weighted at 100%.
At the end of annealing, the allowed component move radius (in Manhattan space) is one millimeter.
Components are also allowed to rotate during placement. We ﬁx the ratio of rotation moves at 25%.
Penalizing the overlap quadratically encourages small overlap between all the components.
The linear term produces better behavior when overlap between two components is less than 1 mm2 (when the edges are barely overlapping).
Our router uses an iterative cost-based rip-up and reroute strategy based on A* search.

After every net has been routed to minimum cost, nets are ripped up (their costs removed from the routing grid) and rerouted, one by one. Ripping up and rerouting all nets once represents one iteration of our router algorithm.
The results for our router, presented in Section 4.10, were routed with 20 iterations.

Because the trace-width is wider than the grid resolution (typically by a factor of 6 or more) we use a partial cost update method for the search-frontier, based on a rasterized circle whose radius is the trace width.
Allowing only laser vias (which are smaller than through-hole vias and only penetrate one layer) results in an approximate 4x speedup over through-hole vias.
Our autorouter prioritizes layout-vs-schematic (LvS) completion over design rule violation (DRV) cleanness
FreeRouting only performs DRV-clean routing, and will not output routes that violate the design rules.
The framework source code is available on GitHub2.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports quantitative routing results for FanoutNet (an RL-based fanout method) versus baselines. FanoutNet "obtains 100% routability in all PCB cases" and achieves RT (%) of 100 across cases when using both spatial and structured features (Pre-RT (Both) = 84/78/85/83/80; RT (Both) = 100/100/100/100/100). Compared to the MCR baseline, FanoutNet shows different via and wire-length patterns: example #Via MCR = [133,164,307,262,337] vs FanoutNet = [113,185,393,302,359]; WL (10^2 mil) MCR = [174,199,480,740,464] vs FanoutNet = [170,228,520,772,477]. Commercial routers Freerouting and ELECTRA attain an average routability of ~99%; FreeRouting reduces wire length and via count but incurs a very long runtime, while ELECTRA "completes the routing in seconds for all test cases but produces higher wire length and via count." FanoutNet requires much longer training time, but inference (generating fanout results) occurs in seconds. Ablation shows fusing spatial and structured features improves pre-routability and final routing. Other methods: GA is slow and less effective; ARL improves quality efficiently; NB-A* prioritizes routability but struggles to balance multi-objective weights in complex cases.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports quantitative runtime comparisons for DREAMPlaceFPGA (the DREAMPlace variant discussed) versus elfPlace-CPU and elfPlace-GPU. DREAMPlaceFPGA achieves 5.35× faster global placement and 1.82× faster overall placement compared to an 8-thread elfPlace-CPU. Versus elfPlace-GPU, DREAMPlaceFPGA is 19% faster for global placement but, because its IO/runtime for parsing and database creation is higher (the ‘IO’ runtime is 2.2× slower than elfPlace-CPU and elfPlace-GPU), it is 1.2% slower for overall placement. The authors attribute the GPU global-placement advantage to a flat database in DREAMPlaceFPGA that allows direct GPU operator calls, whereas elfPlace-GPU must convert custom data structures to flat arrays. The excerpt notes that elfPlace-CPU and elfPlace-GPU achieve similar routed wirelength across benchmarks, but it does not provide numerical wirelength reduction values for DREAMPlace (or DREAMPlaceFPGA) nor any explicit ISPD or ICCAD testcase results in this excerpt.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports quantitative results for DREAMPlace 2.0 on ISPD 2005 benchmarks. Table I (ISPD 2005 contest, float32) shows HPWL and runtime breakdowns per design; for example adaptec1 reports nearly identical HPWL (RePlAce GP 73.23 vs DREAMPlace GP 73.19) with total runtimes reduced (RePlAce total 118s vs DREAMPlace total 84s in the CPU 40-thread comparison). The paper states that DREAMPlace achieves a 1.3× speedup over RePlAce when using 40 CPU threads and achieves about 14× speedup when using the GPU (stated both as "14× speedup on GPU" and "more than 14× speedup over the state-of-the-art RePlAce using 40 CPU threads"). Solver comparisons note Nesterov gives similar solution quality to Adam but is ~2× faster in GP, while SGD with momentum yields about 1.2% worse wirelength than Nesterov/Adam. Overall, DREAMPlace matches or very closely matches RePlAce wirelength on ISPD testcases while substantially reducing runtime, especially with GPU acceleration.
contexts.question: What are the quantitative results for DREAMPlace GPU-accelerated placement, including wirelength reduction, runtime speedup over CPU placers, and benchmark results on ISPD or ICCAD testcases?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes two DREAMPlaceFPGA-PL pack-legalize variants (ϑ1: Sg=|ς|/16, Rt=|ς|/8; ϑ2: Sg=|ς|/8, Rt=|ς|/4) and reports quantitative effects vs. elfPlace CPU implementation. Variant ϑ1 yields the largest DL (direct legalization) acceleration: 9.5× and 7.0× faster than 8- and 16-thread elfPlace for the DL step, and reduces overall pack-legalize time by 2.8× and 2.2× versus 8- and 16-thread elfPlace. ϑ2 gives smaller runtime gains but less impact on solution quality. Reported quality deltas: ϑ2 increases placement HPWL and routed wirelength by 0.3% and 0.6% on average; ϑ1 increases HPWL by 0.4% and routed wirelength by 0.9%. The document groups GPU global placer + GPU pack-legalizer as DREAMPlaceFPGA GP + PL and states Table 3.2 compares HPWL, routed WL (ℜ-WL) and stage runtimes. The excerpt does not provide full end-to-end runtime speedups for the complete placement flow nor explicit ISPD/ICCAD benchmark-by-benchmark results within these pages.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports that DREAMPlace 2.0 provides multi-threaded and GPU-accelerated global placement (GP) and detailed placement (DP) for weighted wirelength minimization, with a CPU/GPU choice for users. The implementation pairs Python-level algorithms with highly optimized C++/CUDA low-level operators and was evaluated on a machine with two Intel 20-core Gold 6230 CPUs (40 cores) and one NVIDIA RTX 2080Ti GPU. Experiments shown in the paper use the ISPD 2005 contest benchmarks. A quantitative runtime claim in the excerpt is that DREAMPlace achieves a 1.3× speedup over RePlAce when using 40 CPU threads, with “no quality degradation.” The excerpt does not provide numeric wirelength-reduction percentages, per-benchmark HPWL or displacement numbers, wall-clock runtimes, or ICCAD testcase results. It also does not give detailed ISPD/ICCAD table results or comparisons beyond the stated 1.3× overall speedup against RePlAce (with NTUplace3 for legalization).
contexts.question: What are the quantitative results for DREAMPlace GPU-accelerated placement, including wirelength reduction, runtime speedup over CPU placers, and benchmark results on ISPD or ICCAD testcases?

contexts.context: The excerpt reports quantitative comparisons on ISPD'2016 benchmarks for GPU-accelerated placers. DREAMPlaceFPGA (implemented on a deep-learning toolkit) attains 1.08× faster global placer runtime than elfPlace-GPU and 4.77× faster than elfPlace-CPU. elfPlace-GPU itself achieves 4.4× speedup over a 20-thread elfPlace-CPU. However, both elfPlace-GPU and DREAMPlaceFPGA incur small HPWL degradations of 0.1% and 0.5% respectively versus elfPlace-CPU. IO overhead is significant for DREAMPlaceFPGA: IO accounts for ≈7% of total placement runtime (versus 1.7% and 3.1% for elfPlace-CPU and elfPlace-GPU), and the fixed intermediate DB (FIdB) is 28% faster than a CIdB but ≈2.2× slower in IO than elfPlace. Database format differences explain runtime gaps ("the 19% runtime difference for global placement"). Routing runtimes: elfPlace-GPU shows a 9% higher routing runtime than elfPlace-CPU; DREAMPlaceFPGA routing runtime is similar to elfPlace-GPU (≈1% higher) and ≈10% higher than elfPlace-CPU. For a congested case (FPGA05) DREAMPlaceFPGA produced placement causing a 20% higher Vivado routing runtime, though across 12 benchmarks DREAMPlaceFPGA had lower routing runtime on 8 of them. Vivado routing of FPGA05 took >1.5 hours for all placers.
contexts.question: What are the quantitative results for DREAMPlace GPU-accelerated placement, including wirelength reduction, runtime speedup over CPU placers, and benchmark results on ISPD or ICCAD testcases?

contexts.context: The excerpt reports DREAMPlaceFPGA evaluated on the ISPD’2016 contest benchmarks (12 synthetic FPGA designs). Table 2.2 gives per-design placement HPWL and routed wirelength (RWL) for elfPlace-CPU, elfPlace-GPU, and DREAMPlaceFPGA. The authors state that DREAMPlaceFPGA’s global placer "incurs 0.5% and 0.3% higher placement HPWL and routed wirelength than elfPlace-CPU across all the benchmarks due to the implementation differences." elfPlace-CPU and elfPlace-GPU produce very similar HPWL and RWL (elfPlace-GPU only accelerates some operators on GPU). Example per-design entries show close parity (e.g., FPGA01: CPU 273/318, GPU 274/317, DREAMPlaceFPGA 275/318; FPGA08: CPU 7,108/7,661 vs DREAMPlaceFPGA 6,884/7,475; FPGA11: CPU 9,450/10,421 vs DREAMPlaceFPGA 9,360/10,340). A "Geo. Ratio" row shows near-1.00 ratios across placers. The designs were routed with AMD/Xilinx Vivado v2015.4 to measure routability and RWL. The excerpt does not present a clear, attributable single-number runtime speedup of DREAMPlace (or DREAMPlaceFPGA) versus CPU placers—runtime data appears in the surrounding table but is not clearly labeled/parsable in this excerpt.
contexts.question: What are the quantitative results for DREAMPlace GPU-accelerated placement, including wirelength reduction, runtime speedup over CPU placers, and benchmark results on ISPD or ICCAD testcases?

snippets: Table 2.1 lists the instance composition of the ISPD’2016 contest bench-marks [152] that are employed to evaluate DREAMPlaceFPGA against the base-lines.

DREAMPlaceFPGA’s global placer on a deep-learning toolkit incurs 0.5% and 0.3% higher placement HPWL and routed wirelength than elfPlace-CPU across all the benchmarks due to the implementation differences.

The elfPlace-CPU and elfPlace-GPU placers are identical in implementation except for the wirelength and density operators (that are accelerated on GPU in elfPlace-GPU), achieve very similar placement HPWL and RWL across all the benchmarks.

All the placers employed elfPlace-CPU for the packing-legalization and detailed placement stages. After placement, the designs were routed using AMD/Xilinx Vivado v2015.4 [5] to ensure routability and evaluate the routed wirelength.

contexts.context: This excerpt reports quantitative and qualitative outcomes for the Ranking Cost (evolution-based) routing approach. The method scales to large synthetic routing instances: experiments on maps of size 150 × 150 with 15 nets are solved "within 3 min." Success rate improves with increased sampling of ordering permutations, but brute-force sampling is limited because the "order complexity is O(k!), where k is the number of pairs (or nets)." Learned ranking orders outperform randomly sampled orders in success rate. Cost maps trained by the method encode global information and can influence early routes to avoid blocking later nets (guiding routers toward global optima). A post-processing step reduces total connected length while incurring "insigniﬁcant time-consuming." The paper also notes that ranking-parameter learning improves performance compared to cost-map-only learning. The excerpt does not provide explicit numeric values for wirelength, overflow, completion rates, or detailed runtimes beyond the 3-minute scaling example, and contains no direct quantitative comparisons to Liao et al. or to PCB-placement-with-RL works.
contexts.question: What are the quantitative results for RL-based global routing for PCBs, including Liao et al. deep RL routing, Ranking Cost evolution-based routing, and physically constrained PCB placement using deep reinforcement learning? Include metrics on wirelength, overflow, completion rate, and runtime.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports the experimental setup and quantitative success-rate results for the Ranking Cost (evolution-based) routing planner. Experiments used a grid-map simulator with map sizes 16×16, 32×32 and 64×64, containing 4, 6, and 10 start-end pairs respectively; 50 random maps per size/type (simple/obstacle) were generated (300 total). Metrics reported are success rate and the common average total length (average length computed only over maps that all algorithms successfully solved to ensure fairness). Hyperparameters: max training episodes 1000, learning rate 0.001, number of evaluators 40, noise std σr=0.1 and σc=0.1; rewards scaled to [−1,0] with −1 for no connection. Table 3 gives success rates for randomly sampled ranking orders at different sample counts: sample-number success rates vary (e.g., 0.08 at 5 samples, 0.12 at 10, 0.16 at 15/20, up to 0.24 at 200), while the learned ranking achieves 0.30 success rate. The excerpt does not provide wirelength/overflow values, completion rates for Liao et al. deep RL routing, or runtime numbers, nor results for physically constrained PCB placement using deep RL.

contexts.context: The provided excerpt describes Liao et al.'s formulation of global routing as a deep reinforcement learning problem. They frame routing as an MDP and select a Deep Q-Network (DQN) to learn routing policies. A key modeling choice is that a single Q-network can consider nets and pins conjointly rather than routing nets strictly sequentially. The optimization objectives are explicit: minimize total overﬂow (congestion) across edges and minimize total wirelength (WL). The paper reports that their DRL approach "can outperform the benchmark method of a sequential A* method," and they also developed a parameterized global routing problem-set generator for training and evaluation. The excerpt contains no quantitative metrics (wirelength numbers, overflow amounts, completion rates, or runtimes). To answer the question about quantitative results for Liao et al. and other RL-based PCB methods (Ranking Cost evolution–based routing; physically constrained PCB placement via DRL), additional sections or other papers with experimental tables and runtime measurements will be required.

snippets: This work presents a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) driven approach to global routing.
We choose a Deep Q-network (DQN) as our fundamental RL algorithm to address the global routing problem.
can consider the nets and the pins to be routed conjointly, as opposed to attempting to route them sequentially

The goal of global routing is to minimize: (1) The congestion in the final design, which is the total overﬂow accrued over all the edges, and (2) The total wirelength (WL), which is the sum of tile-to-tile connections emanating from all the nets used in the design.
The results indicate that the approach can outperform the benchmark method of a sequential A* method
development of a global routing problem sets generator with the ability to generate parameterized global routing problem sets with different size and constraints,
we model the problem as a Markov Decision Process [22].

contexts.context: Crocker (2021) reports a proof-of-concept RL approach focused on a constrained ring-placement subset of PCB placement. The method produced visually and physically valid ring placements (examples: one run placed 4 components yielding an 8-component board; another placed 8 components yielding a 12-component board) and ensured routability for these ring structures. Training hyperparameters reported include discretization=10, action space=(10 × 10 × 2), policy depth=5 TCNN layers, encoder limit=10 components, entropy bonus=0.005, clipping 𝜖=0.2, learning rate=1e-5, and episodes run=2 million. Limitations highlighted: the encoder was pre-trained up to 10 components and performance degraded beyond ~12 components; the action space explodes for more realistic boards (example: a 100×100 grid × 4 rotations × 2 mirrors × 2 layers → 160,000 placements per component); and learned reward models are vulnerable to reward hacking (the agent found high-reward but invalid ring placements after a few million epochs). The excerpt does not provide standard quantitative metrics (e.g., success rates, routing cost reductions) for broader PCB placement beyond these qualitative/structural results and hyperparameters.
contexts.question: What quantitative results does Liao et al. 2020 deep reinforcement learning approach for global routing report? What results does Ranking Cost report for circuit routing? What results does Crocker 2021 report for physically constrained PCB placement?

snippets: By modifying the methods outlined by Mirhoseini and Goldie et al.  to avoid heuristic reward systems, this exploration produced a series of placements that not only improved with training, but maintained physical validity.
The model places valid rings, ensuring all routing can be completed successfully.
The placements are physically verifiable. No components hang off of edges or overlap other components.
The two placements below were trained with the following parameters:
Discretization         10
Action Space          (10 × 10 × 2)
Policy Depth         5 TCNN Layers
Encoder Limit        10 Components
Entropy Bonus        0.005
Clipping 𝜖              0.2
Learning Rate      1𝑒−5
Episodes Run         2 Million
In this AI generated placement, the agent successfully placed 4 components resulting in a board with 8 components total.
In this AI generated placement, the agent successfully placed 8 components resulting in a board with 12 components total.
In this thesis, the encoder was trained on up to 10 components, which resulted in a loss in performance beyond 12 component placements.

Considering a more detailed board with a 100 × 100 location grid, 4 rotation options, 2 mirror options, and 2 placement layers, the action space expands to 160, 000 possible placements for each component.
In a few million epochs the agent was able to hack the learned reward system and formed placements that were invalid rings, but received high reward.

contexts.context: The provided excerpt (Liao et al., 2020) describes a Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) approach for IC global routing that models routing as a Markov Decision Process and uses a Deep Q-Network (DQN). The method emphasizes conjoint optimization: a single, properly trained Q-network can consider multiple nets and pins jointly rather than routing nets sequentially. The paper defines the global routing objectives as minimizing total edge overflow (congestion) and total wirelength (WL). The authors also present a parameterized global routing problem-set generator to create varied training and evaluation instances. The excerpt states that the approach "can outperform the benchmark method of a sequential A* method," but the excerpt does not include any quantitative metrics, numeric results, tables, or specific performance numbers (e.g., percent improvement, wirelength/overflow values, runtimes) for the DRL method. No information about "Ranking Cost" results for circuit routing or Crocker 2021 results for physically constrained PCB placement is present in the excerpt.

snippets: the results indicate that the approach can outperform the benchmark method of a sequential A* method, suggesting a promising potential for deep reinforcement learning for global routing and other routing or path planning problems in general.

The goal of global routing is to minimize: (1) The congestion in the final design, which is the total overﬂow accrued over all the edges, and (2) The total wirelength (WL), which is the sum of tile-to-tile connections emanating from all the nets used in the design.
We choose a Deep Q-network (DQN) as our fundamental RL algorithm to address the global routing problem.
a properly trained Q-network within our DQN formulation can consider the nets and the pins to be routed conjointly, as opposed to attempting to route them sequentially

Another major contribution of this work is the development of a global routing problem sets generator with the ability to generate parameterized global routing problem sets with different size and constraints

contexts.context: The excerpt reports quantitative results for the Ranking Cost (RC) circuit-routing method. Table 1 reports success rate and average total length (bracketed) over 6 seeds. Ranking Cost (two variants: I without post-processing, II with post-processing) achieves the best performance on most maps, including reaching 100% connecting rate on 16×16 (4 pairs). Example entries show high success rates and compact lengths (e.g., Ranking Cost I/II: 1.0±0.0 (36.5±0.02) and 1.0±0.0 (108.2±0.32) for smaller and medium maps; on the hardest setting (64×64 with obstacles and 10 pairs) Ranking Cost II reports 0.32±0.0 (358.5±0.0)). The authors note that ranking-parameter learning improves over Cost-Map Learning. Runtime (wallclock) is reported as acceptable: Ranking Cost variants take about 38.3 sec/map (longer than pure A* but yielding better solutions).

contexts.context: The excerpt reports quantitative and qualitative results from Liao et al. 2020 on using a DQN-based router for global routing (Type II problems). Using random memory burn-in, the DQN router produced smaller wirelength (WL) than A* in only 57.5% of cases. Switching the burn-in memory to A*-based solutions improved DQN performance: "DQN routing solutions are mostly better than corresponding A* routing solutions." The tuned DQN model yielded better solutions on 8×8×2 problems and generalized to 16×16×2 problems without changing model parameters or architecture. Results are presented and compared using WL metrics (DQN vs. A*). The paper concludes that the proposed DQN router produces superior solutions over A* in most Type II cases after tuning (batch size, learning rate, network structures, ε, γ, and memory burn-in). The excerpt does not contain any information about Ranking Cost's results for circuit routing or Crocker 2021's results for physically constrained PCB placement.

contexts.context: The provided excerpt contains partial quantitative information. It reports that Liao et al. (2020) evaluated their deep reinforcement learning global-routing approach only on very small maps: "the maps with the size of 8 × 8." For the Ranking Cost method (this paper), experiments include: success-rate curves on maps "with size of 64 × 64 and with obstacles," showing that the "learned ranking order can achieve much better performance than randomly sampled orders." The authors also test scalability by applying Ranking Cost to much larger instances: "maps with the size of 150 × 150 and with 15 pairs," and state "Our algorithm solves such large maps within 3 min." The paper further reports that cost maps learned by the method encode high-cost regions that steer routers away from blocking other nets ("There are two larger cost values (the yellow grids) ... which will give out a large cost if the path chooses to go through here.") and that a post-processing step "can help to reduce the total connected length with insigniﬁcant time-consuming." The excerpt does not provide numerical success-rate values, throughput, wirelength, or other scalar metrics for Liao et al. or Crocker (2021); no information about Crocker 2021 appears in the excerpt.

contexts.context: Evidence summary: The excerpt reports an RL placement proof-of-concept for small “ring” PCBs that produced physically valid placements (e.g., “The model places valid rings…,” nondeterministic exploration, no overlaps). Training hyperparameters: Discretization 10; Action Space (10×10×2); Policy Depth 5 TCNN layers; Encoder Limit 10 components; Entropy Bonus 0.005; Clipping ϵ=0.2; Learning Rate 1e−5; Episodes Run 2 million. Limits: encoder trained to 10 components fails beyond ~12; action space explodes (100×100 grid, 4 rotations, 2 mirrors, 2 layers → 160,000 placements/component); routing is “computationally prohibitive” except for rings. Learned reward nets are vulnerable to “Reward Hacking”; true rewards must run in milliseconds.

contexts.context: Paper excerpt reports DQN-based routing experiments on 40 benchmark problems. Tuned hyperparameters: batch size ∈ {32,64,128} (optimal 32), learning rates {1e-3, 1e-4, 1e-5} (1e-4 best), network nodes [32,64,32], ε=0.05 (with decay from 0.05→0.01 after 2000 episodes ineffective), and discount γ ∈ {1,0.95,0.9,0.8} (optimal γ=0.9). After ~2000 episodes feasible solutions appear; larger batch (128) and large γ cause reward oscillations. Quantitative: DQN yields WL improvement over A* in 80% (γ=1), 90% (0.95), 97.5% (0.9), 95% (0.8) of cases; “OF” for DQN is zero in all cases while A* sometimes has non-zero OF. Burn-in: A*-based memory selected.

contexts.context: Paper proposes a learned Ranking-Cost routing using OpenAI-ES: reward "F(θr, θc) = −L(Rθr, Cθc)" and normalized evaluator rewards "ri = (ri − rmean)/rstd". It trains cost maps and a ranking parameter, with a "post-processing step" that re-plans paths via A*. Tests on 300 grid maps (16×16, 32×32, 64×64) with/without obstacles. Results: Ranking Cost I/II reach 1.0 success (36.5 avg length) on 16×16; 1.0 (≈108.2) on 32×32; on hardest 64×64 with obstacles achieve ≈0.32 success and lengths ≈358–360. Runtime ≈38.3 sec/map (Seq A*(5)=0.8s, Seq A*(200)=41.5s, VINs=1.4s).

contexts.context: This excerpt describes DeepTH, a three-head policy network for chip placement that includes a reconstruction head to recover a visual representation of the placement. Input object features and the adjacency matrix are used; both the real canvas B and reconstructed canvas B̂ are fed into a function f and a reconstruction error LRec = ||f(B̂) − f(B)||2 is computed. PPO is used to train the policy network. HPWL (half-perimeter wire length) is the placement metric; six ISPD 2005 benchmarks are used for evaluation. The reconstruction error (LRec) is used as an intrinsic exploration bonus during training, and the final reward is the sum of HPWL and this intrinsic reward. Results reported: DeepTH achieves an average 19.02% HPWL improvement over DREAMPlace, and is 28.47% and 19.89% better than Google’s placer and DeepPlace respectively. The paper also notes DeepTH solutions are overlap-free whereas SOTA DeepPlace is not. The excerpt does not report runtimes or detailed per-benchmark HPWL numbers.
contexts.question: What are the quantitative results for ChiPFormer transferable chip placement, chip placement with diffusion, DeepTH three-head chip placement, and Bayesian optimization for macro placement? Include HPWL, runtime, and comparison metrics.

contexts.context: The excerpt provides quantitative results for ChiPFormer. For macro placement (Table 2, HPWL in ×10^5), ChiPFormer shows strong improvements: e.g., adaptec1 MaskPlace(3k) = 7.62±0.67 vs ChiPFormer(300) = 7.02±0.11 (−7.87%) and ChiPFormer(2k) = 6.62±0.05 (−13.12%). The paper reports that ChiPFormer(300) attains minimal wirelength in 10/12 circuits while using 10× fewer rollouts than MaskPlace(3k), and ChiPFormer(2k) reaches state-of-the-art on all circuits. For mixed-size placement (Table 3, HPWL in ×10^7), ChiPFormer(workflow) for adaptec1 is 6.45±0.02 (−0.31% vs best baseline), and for adaptec3 visualization shows ChiPFormer HPWL = 13.97×10^7 with zero overlap. The method uses offline data collection (500 expert-level placements per circuit) and the authors state offline finetuning yields more stable performance and more than 10× speed-up in time efficiency compared to prior online methods. contexts.context: The excerpt reports quantitative results for Liu et al. (2021) global placement with deep-learning-enabled routability optimization. They define a congestion metric (Congestion Rate, CR) and use Routed Wirelength (WL) as performance metrics. Their routability prediction network achieves high fidelity: "more than 90% of the test cases are in good performance with the metrics in [17] (NRMS < 0.2; SSIM > 0.8)." Table II compares NTUplace4dr, DREAMPlace, and the authors' method across ISPD 2015 benchmarks. Averaged over benchmarks, NTUplace4dr reports H-CR=0.131, V-CR=0.069, WL=8.94 (e+06 um), RT=2102.82s; DREAMPlace reports H-CR=0.141, V-CR=0.091, WL=7.68 (e+06 um), RT=22.28s; the proposed method reports H-CR=0.124, V-CR=0.087, WL=7.27 (e+06 um), RT=91.13s. Notably, on the pathological matrix_mult_c benchmark the proposed approach reduces H-CR from 48.956 (NTUplace4dr) to 0.029 and WL from 126.71e6 um to 3.42e6 um, demonstrating strong robustness. The placer is gradient-driven (uses ∇xJ, ∇yJ) and propagates gradients through routability estimators. The excerpt does not contain results for DRC-aware routing or Murphy (2020) PCB neural-network fitness function.
contexts.question: What are the quantitative results and key findings for global placement with deep learning routability optimization (Liu et al. 2021), DRC-aware routing, and neural network fitness function for PCB design automation (Murphy 2020)? Definition 1. (Congestion Rate - CR) CR shows the average lack of routing resources after global routing.
Also, we use the classic metric, Routed Wirelength, WL, to measure the routing performance.
Fig. 4 plots the evaluation of our proposed routability prediction network, where more than 90% of the test cases are in good performance with the metrics in [17] (NRMS < 0.2; SSIM > 0.8).
Our proposed placer utilizes gradients (∇xJ, ∇yJ) to adjust all movable cells and optimize the objective function.

contexts.context: Liu et al. (2021) develop a routability-driven placer using a deep learning model (fully convolutional networks) integrated into the placement objective to explicitly guide cell movement. Evaluation on modified ISPD2015 benchmarks shows up to 9.05% reduction in congestion rate (reported versus DREAMPlace) and a reported 18.68% reduction versus NTUplace4dr in one statement; routed wirelength is reduced by 5.30% compared with DREAMPlace and NTUplace4dr. The method provides efficient and relatively accurate routing congestion modeling at placement. Runtime: average runtime is about 4× slower than DREAMPlace but roughly 23× faster than NTUplace4dr due to native GPU acceleration. Runtime breakdown (Fig. 5) highlights that computing congestion penalty gradients ∇L is the most time-consuming step (46.10% of total runtime), computing L (features extraction, inference, l2-norm) takes 17.23%, and the l2-norm operator in PyTorch alone accounts for 13.6% and is noted as optimizable. The approach handles non-differentiability by discrete computation of some components.

contexts.context: Murphy (2020) evaluates neural-network-based routability predictors against simple metrics (HPWL, crossings) and reports substantially lower relative-routability error rates for the learned models. For single-board experiments (Boards 1 and 2) the learned models outperform HPWL and crossings; error rates fall rapidly as the routability-difference between compared placements increases (for Board 1 the error rate reaches zero once the routability difference exceeds 0.04). Board 2 is harder: routability differences must exceed 0.08 for error rates to drop below 1%. In multiboard (no novel board) training, the network predicting absolute routability attains MAE 0.0182 on the combined test set (per-board MAEs: Board1 0.0112, Board2 0.0235, Board3 0.013, Board4 0.0318, Board5 0.0118). For the novel-board experiment (Board 6), the multiboard-trained model has very high absolute MAE on Board 6 (0.2205) but a relative-routability error of 0.1838, indicating it learned features useful for ranking placements. Fine-tuning with small Board 6 datasets greatly reduces absolute MAE (e.g., to ~0.0128/0.0123 with 25/50 examples) while relative error remains around 0.18–0.19, with modest improvements as more examples or layer freezing are used.

contexts.context: Liu et al. (2021) propose a fully convolutional network (FCN) that predicts routing congestion maps from placement solutions and integrate this prediction into the DREAMPlace global placer as an explicit congestion penalty. Their approach directly injects the learned congestion estimate into the placement objective to produce more route-friendly placements. On ISPD2015 benchmarks, they report that their method can achieve up to 9.05% reduction in congestion rate and a 5.30% reduction in routed wirelength compared with the state-of-the-art. The paper highlights the FCN-based prediction model and its integration into the placement objective as key contributions that enable explicit routability optimization within a GPU-accelerated placer.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports quantitative runtime and some proxy-cost results from Cheng et al. (ISPD 2023) comparing Circuit Training (CT) to CMP, RePlAce, simulated annealing (SA) and AutoDMP on three NG45 enablements. Table 8 shows CT training runtimes: Ariane-NG45 32.31 hours, BlackParrot-NG45 50.51 hours, and MemPool-NG45 81.23 hours. By contrast, CMP and RePlAce complete in minutes to a few hours (e.g., Ariane: CMP 0.05 h, RePlAce 0.06 h; BlackParrot: CMP 0.33 h, RePlAce 2.52 h; MemPool: CMP 1.97 h, RePlAce N.A.), SA was run with a budgeted stop of 12.5 hours per run, and AutoDMP runtimes were modest (Ariane 0.29 h, BlackParrot 0.71 h, MemPool 1.73 h). The paper notes CT uses substantially more compute: CT training used eight NVIDIA V100 GPUs plus two collect servers (each with 96 CPUs running 13 collect jobs). SA was run as 320 parallel jobs (1 CPU thread each). The excerpt also reports proxy costs for the final CT solution on Ariane: proxy wirelength 0.0913, proxy density 0.5290, and proxy congestion 0.9017. The document states CT consumes substantially more runtime/compute than other placers. The excerpt does not include explicit HPWL/post-route wirelength numeric comparisons across placers or detailed benchmark HPWL deltas.

contexts.context: The provided excerpt documents methodology and comparative setup from Cheng et al. (updated assessment). It describes Circuit Training (CT) as an RL method that sequentially places macros and defines the proxy cost used for RL reward as composed of wirelength, density, and congestion. The paper evaluates three CT variants: CT-Scratch (train from scratch), CT-AC (fine-tune Google’s released checkpoint), and CT-Ours (fine-tune the authors' own pre-trained checkpoint). Classical placers used for comparison include an improved simulated-annealing (SA) baseline (multi-threaded, go-with-the-winners enhancements), RePlAce (electrostatic model from OpenROAD), Cadence CMP (commercial placer), and human-expert placements. The authors report that their improved SA can yield up to 26% better proxy cost within the same runtime while using only one-quarter of the resources versus a prior SA implementation, and that SA and human baselines remain superior to the latest AlphaChip while using substantially fewer resources. They also doubled CT iterations from 200 to 400 to aid convergence and added sub-10nm testcase enablements. The excerpt does not include detailed per-benchmark numerical tables for HPWL, routed wirelength, runtimes, or explicit benchmark-by-benchmark comparisons.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports qualitative and some quantitative comparisons in the updated assessment of Circuit Training (AlphaChip) versus classical baselines. Key numeric details: a pre-trained CT-AC model used 20 diverse TPU blocks; CT-Ariane-X4 is a large testcase with 532 macros (TSMC 7nm). Pre-training behavior: the model pre-trained on other slices shows a sharp placement-return jump at 50 hours but only ~4% further improvement over the next 125 hours. Table XI and Fig. 6 (discussed) link pre-training dataset diversity and number of macros to convergence (examples show Max #Macro values like 324 and 133 across slices). The authors trained AlphaChip from scratch and fine-tuned the released checkpoint, and they strengthened the simulated annealing (SA) baseline (multi-threading + a 1994 “go-with-the-winners” metaheuristic). Their experiments report that SA achieves better results than AlphaChip on CT-Ariane-X4 in both post-detailed placement HPWL and CT proxy cost while using a fraction of the runtime and compute. They also note that the CT proxy objective correlates weakly with final post-route PPA metrics. The excerpt does not provide explicit numeric HPWL, wirelength, runtime, or full benchmark tables comparing Circuit Training, RePlAce, and other placers.

contexts.context: Cheng et al. (ISPD 2023) provide quantitative comparisons between Circuit Training (CT, RL-based) and other placers. They report final-proxy-cost numbers for one run: proxy wirelength = 0.0913, proxy density = 0.5290, proxy congestion = 0.9017. Overall, human experts and simulated annealing (SA) often outperform the RL framework: on ICCAD04 benchmarks SA beats CT on proxy cost in all cases and on HPWL in 16 of 17 cases. On modern benchmarks, SA beats CT on proxy cost in 4 of 5 cases and on routed wirelength in 5 of 6 cases. CT narrowly outperforms RePlAce on modern testcases for proxy cost and the Nature Table 1 metrics when CT uses an initial physical-synthesis placement; in 6 head-to-head comparisons RePlAce still wins 3/6 routed wirelength comparisons and 2/6 total power comparisons. Against prior “Stronger Baselines” results, Cheng et al. confirm SA outperforms CT on ICCAD04 (CT wins only 1/17 HPWL comparisons) and note that RePlAce beats CT on ICCAD04 where CT did not win any proxy cost or HPWL comparisons. They also note pre-training effects: the Nature paper showed pre-training benefits mainly for runtime and final proxy cost, and Cheng et al. used eight NVIDIA V100 GPUs with global batch size 1024 to reproduce CT runs.

snippets: Our proxy wirelength cost, proxy density cost and proxy congestion cost of the final solution are respectively 0.0913, 0.5290 and 0.9017.
The solutions typically produced by human experts and SA are superior to those generated by the RL framework in the majority of cases we tested.
For ICCAD04 [21], in terms of proxy cost, SA beats CT in all cases and in terms of HPWL, SA beats CT in 16 out of 17 cases.
For modern benchmarks, in terms of proxy cost, SA beats CT in four out of five cases, and in terms of routed wirelength, SA beats CT in five out of six cases.

We confirmed that Circuit Training [23] (narrowly) beats RePlAce [22] on modern testcases with respect to both proxy cost and Nature Table 1 metrics when it uses the initial placement as shown in Figure 2.
(Out of 6 head-to-head comparisons for each available metric, RePlAce wins only 3/6 routed wirelength comparisons and 2/6 total power comparisons.)
We confirmed that SA outperforms Circuit Training on ICCAD04 benchmarks [21]. Out of 17 head-to-head comparisons for each available metric, Circuit Training wins 1/17 HPWL comparisons.

For Stronger Baselines: We confirmed that RePlAce beats Circuit Training on ICCAD04 benchmarks. Out of 17 head-to-head comparisons for each available metric, Circuit Training did not win any proxy cost or HPWL comparisons.
The Nature paper [9] did not show benefits from pre-training for Table 1 metrics. [9] only shows benefits (from the pre-trained model) in terms of runtime and final proxy cost.
We use eight NVIDIA-V100 GPUs to train the model for global batch size = 1024.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports quantitative results for the authors' RL-based macro placement (DeepPlace) on ISPD-2005 circuits and compares it to a sequential learning placer and DREAMPlace/RePlace. Table 2 lists total wirelength (presumably HPWL or routing wirelength) for several circuits: adaptec1 — DeepPlace 80,117,232 vs Sequential 86,675,688 vs DREAMPlace 83,749,616; adaptec3 — DeepPlace 241,072,304 vs Sequential 257,956,784 vs DREAMPlace 264,789,408; bigblue1 — DeepPlace 140,435,296 vs Sequential 168,456,768 (DREAMPlace: congestion check failure); bigblue3 — DeepPlace 450,633,360 vs Sequential 460,581,568 (DREAMPlace: congestion check failure); bigblue4 — DeepPlace 951,984,128 vs Sequential 1,039,976,064 (DREAMPlace: congestion check failure). The authors note DREAMPlace often fails congestion checks (likely due to macro overlap) while DeepPlace and the sequential RL placer produce viable placements. Runtime: the joint method achieves approximately a 4× speedup over RePlace in total runtime. Pretraining helps: pretraining the policy on a macro-only netlist for ~300 iterations produced the best results (500 iterations led to overfitting and worse final placement). For joint placement+routing (DeepPR) the RL agent significantly improves final routed wirelength compared with a separate placement-then-routing pipeline, and the authors argue HPWL is not always an accurate proxy for routed wirelength. The excerpt does not contain comparisons to simulated annealing or a 2023 assessment; it focuses on the 2021 DeepPlace/DeepPR results and runtime comparisons to RePlace/DREAMPlace.

contexts.context: The excerpt from Cheng et al. (ISPD 2023) reports comparative evaluation of Google’s Circuit Training (CT) reinforcement-learning-based macro placer against simulated annealing (SA) and other flows. The authors study alternative proxy cost weightings (three settings: γ=0.5,λ=0.5; γ=1.0,λ=0.5; γ=0.01,λ=0.01) to assess sensitivity. A key finding cited here is that CT vs. SA behavior is stable across proxy-cost weightings and that “Table 7 shows that SA consistently achieves lower proxy cost than CT for ibm09 and ibm15 across the different weight combinations.” The paper uses a proxy cost combining wirelength, density, and congestion evaluated with force-directed (FD) placement (CT later replaces FD with DREAMPlace). The excerpt includes an ibm15 result line tied to SA (numeric row present) but does not provide full tabulated HPWL, routed wirelength, runtime, or detailed per-benchmark numeric summaries. Repositories and benchmarks used (ICCAD04, MacroPlacement, RePlAce, Circuit Training) are listed, indicating available artifacts to reproduce full quantitative comparisons, but the excerpt itself lacks complete HPWL, wirelength, runtime, and overall benchmark tables.

contexts.context: Murphy (2020) develops a neural-network–based fitness function to predict routability of PCB placements so it can be used as the scoring function in iterative-improvement placement optimizers. Routability is defined as the completion rate a placement would achieve when routed. The work generates a labeled dataset of over 75,000 placed PCB designs derived from six different boards (data generation used AWS cloud resources). Labels include routability measurements (the thesis includes routability-assignment variance tests where each design was routed 100 times). The experimental pipeline explores baseline feature representations and CNN-based image features (including experiments with VGG16), and evaluates models on single-board and multi-board prediction tasks. Models are assessed for both absolute routability prediction and relative routability ranking. The thesis reports that the neural network predictors provide improvement over traditional routability proxies (e.g., wire length and connection-crossing estimates), demonstrating feasibility of using learned routability estimates as placement scoring functions for optimization-based PCB placement automation.

snippets: My team’s goal is to fully automate the Printed Circuit Board (PCB) design process.

The primary objective of the placement stage is to place components in a way that leads to a successful routing stage, which is determined by how many total circuit connections can be satisfied without violating any design rules.
My team currently focuses on iterative improvement approaches to PCB placement automation.
their performance heavily depends on how strongly this scoring function correlates with routability, which is defined as the completion rate that would result if the placement were routed.
Existing routability proxies include wire length and connection crossings estimates, however these only correlate roughly with routability.

This thesis details the development of a neural network that predicts the routability of a placed design, with the end goal of using it as the scoring function in iterative improvment placement alogirthms.
A dataset of over 75,000 placed PCB designs derived from six different PCBs was generated to train this network.
Experiments were performed for both single board and multi board prediction tasks.
Networks were evaluated on their ability to predict both absolute and relative routability, and in both domains improvement over traditional routability proxies is demonstrated.
Overview of the data generation process and how AWS cloud resources are utilized.
Routability assignment variance tests. Each design was routed 100 times, and displayed are the resulting mean routability scores and standard deviations.

contexts.context: Murphy (2020) argues that common heuristics used as placement fitness functions (e.g., normalized crossings, HPWL) do not correlate strongly enough with true routability: routability spans large ranges for fixed heuristic values, and this spread is much larger than the measured stochastic routing variance (Board 1 SD ≈ 0.0053, Board 2 SD ≈ 0.0111 from 5 designs routed 100 times). To improve fitness evaluation, Murphy trained a deep neural network to predict routability from a placed PCB representation using supervised learning. The training dataset comprises placements from six boards (three small <100 components, three large >300 components). Because the GO-based auto-placer used to generate placements explored only a subset of the placement space, a data augmenter was implemented that applies translations, rotations, and swaps to broaden coverage; roughly half of placements are augmented. Dataset sizes per board are reported (training sets: e.g., Board1 12,136; Board4 20,878; test sets listed as well). Murphy expects the network to learn nuanced latent features that better match true routability than traditional heuristics, though the dataset of six boards is acknowledged as limited and future work will expand it.

contexts.context: Murphy (2020) addresses automating PCB placement by learning a placement scoring function that correlates with routability (the fraction of net connections that can be routed without rule violations). The thesis focuses on iterative-improvement placement algorithms, where scoring heavily influences optimization. Existing proxies (wire length, estimated crossings) correlate only roughly with routability; Murphy develops a neural network to predict routability from placed designs to serve as a fitness function. A dataset of over 75,000 placed PCB designs derived from six different PCBs was generated (data-generation pipeline and AWS cloud resources are documented). Experiments cover single-board and multi-board prediction tasks; networks are trained/evaluated to predict both absolute routability and relative routability (ranking placements). The work includes baseline feature representations and CNN architectures (and image-feature experiments including VGG16), plus routability-variance tests (each design routed 100 times). The reported outcome is that the neural-network predictor improves over traditional routability proxies, demonstrating feasibility of using learned routability estimates as placement scoring functions for optimization-based PCB design automation.

contexts.context: Murphy (2020) trains convolutional neural networks to predict PCB routability and tests their use as fitness functions for placement optimization. Methodology: networks were trained on multi-board data (Boards 1–5) and then three variants were ﬁnetuned on a novel PCB (Board 6) using small sets of 25 and 50 Board 6 examples: (1) the pretrained model, (2) the pretrained model with convolutional layers frozen, and (3) the same architecture with randomly initialized weights. Evaluation used absolute routability (MAE) and relative routability (ranking/classification) tasks. Datasets: Table 9 lists dataset sizes for Boards 1–6 (test set for Board 6 = 1,663). Key findings: finetuning dramatically reduced absolute MAE on Board 6 (original MAE 0.2205 -> 0.0123 with 25 points), but provided only minor gains for relative routability. Because placement algorithms (e.g., GO) rely on relative scoring, improved absolute calibration does not necessarily translate to better placement outcomes. A randomly initialized model ﬁnetuned on few Board 6 points matched the relative performance of models trained on Boards 1–5. Future work recommended larger/more varied datasets, integration into placement algorithms, and improving relative routability prediction and non-image features.

Each model was trained on a set of 25 Board 6 data points as well as a set of 50 in order to determine how performance varies when the number of data points increases.
The first network used in these ﬁnetuning experiments was the one trained on Boards 1-5 from section 4.4.1.
the convolutional layers were frozen during ﬁnetuning to see if ﬁxing the feature extraction that occur’s in these layers improves performance.
a network with the same architecture as the previous two models but with all weights randomly intialized was also trained.
The first thing we note is that the absolute routability MAE score on the Board 6 dataset dramatically decreases when the model is ﬁnetuned on Board 6 data points.
The original MAE was 0.2205, and when this same model is ﬁnetuned on just 25 data points this decreases to 0.0123.

the relative relationships are what guide the optimization process in iterative improvement placement algorithms like GO, so this decrease in absolute routability does not necessarily indicate a more successful placement algorithm.
relative routability score improvements are not nearly as signiﬁcant
models ﬁnetuned on 25 Board 6 data points do slightly worse than the one that is not ﬁnetuned, and those ﬁnetuned on 50 data points do only slightly better.

randomly initialized model performed quite similarly to the models that were trained Boards 1-5, indicating that ﬁnetuning a randomly initialized model on a small number of Board 6 data points is enough to match the relative routability performance of a model trained only on Boards 1-5.

It was showed that the network’s routability predictions correlated more strongly with true routability than the wire length and crossings heuristics that have traditionally been used as routabiity proxies.

The network’s performance on the absolute routability task was initially quite poor, however, after ﬁnetuning on a small number of data points of the novel PCB for which it was to be evaluated on, the performance increased dramatically.

Finetuning only slightly increased performance on the relative routability task, however. Because iterative improvement placement algorithms like GO are only concerned with the relative scoring of placements, this result indicates that ﬁnetuning would likely not improve the success of these algorithms.
Table 9: Dataset Sizes

Future work will focus on increasing the size and number of datasets, and increasing the variability of placed designs in these datasets. Additionally, tests that incorporate the trained networks into placement algorithms and evaluate the quality of the generated placements will be run.

The placement stage is important because it impacts the success of the routing stage, which is primarily determined by the completion rate, the percentage of netlist connections that are satisﬁed after routing.

contexts.context: Murphy (2020) argues that standard heuristics (e.g., HPWL, crossings) only partially correlate with true PCB placement routability and have "room for improvement." To address this, he trains a supervised deep neural network that takes a placed-PCB representation as input and predicts a routability score, with the aim of learning latent features more predictive of routability than simple heuristics. Data were generated primarily from a GO-based auto-placer across six boards (three small <100 components and three complex >300 components). Because the auto-placer explored only a limited portion of placement space, Murphy augmented generated placements by applying tunable translations, rotations, and swaps; roughly half the dataset is augmented. Routability labels come from routing runs; variance analysis (100 routings per design) found mean standard deviations of routability around 0.0053 (Board 1) and 0.0111 (Board 2), while heuristic-conditioned routability ranges (e.g., 0.83–0.94 at a fixed crossings value) are much larger, indicating heuristics cannot explain the spread. Dataset sizes are reported in Table 2. Murphy concludes a neural-network fitness function can better match true routability and improve placement optimization, but notes the dataset is limited and should be expanded in future work.
